“She was a victim in this situation,” Brown, now a criminal justice reform advocate, said of Lewis. “Not only is she going to have to serve time in a facility but, over the next five years, anything that she does can trigger her having to serve a 20-year sentence. So,she’s not truly free.”

search_result.title: Coachella 2026 live updates on special guests, from Becky G to Nav
search_result.text:
2:58 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update BIGBANG reunites at Coachella for first show since 2017 Shad Powers Known as one of the bands that helped usher in the K-pop boom, BIGBANG reunited Sunday night at Coachella to perform a show together for the first time since 2017, though the band was scheduled to play Coachella in 2020 before it was wiped out by the COVID pandemic. The three primary members — G-Dragon, Taeyang and Daesung — were back together with their high-energy antics, dipping in and out of Korean and English throughout the set that had a large crowd enrapt and singing every lyric at the Outdoor Theatre. At times during their hour-long set, the three members also did solo performances as each also has had a substantial solo career. This won't be the last time they will perform together. The Coachella set is the unofficial starting point of a worldwide tour, but the dates and cities have not yet been revealed.

2:42 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update Anyma takes Do Lab stage, doesn't immediately address Friday cancellation Paul Albani-Burgio Anyma finally took the stage at Coachella Sunday night, nearly 48 hours after originally scheduled. But it wasn't the Main Stage the Italian-American DJ was playing but rather the Do LaB after a B2B set featuring Anyma and Marlon Hoffstadt was added to the lineup for the space that serves as the festival's EDM epicenter. Anyma, of course, had originally been scheduled to debut a much-hyped new show Friday before wind and safety concerns led Coachella to cancel the performance. The DJ posted a lengthy message to fans on social media in which he said that the show's debut would now come during Weekend 2 because there was not a time slot available on the Main Stage to perform it. Many in the large crowd, which was spilling beyond the immediate Do LaB area, had their phones out to capture the moment as Anyma and Hoffstadt began their set. Twenty five minutes into what was scheduled to be a 75 minute set, neither Anyma or Hoffstadt, who appeared to be all smiles, had spoken a word into their mics leaving fans to wait and see if Anyma would say anything further about the cancellation.

1:47 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update Karol G brings out Mariah Angeliq, Becky G, Wisin Niki Kottmann Karol G knows how to make an entrance. Although she was 30 minutes late, she wasted no time with her history-making set, and brought out her first special guest, Mariah Angeliq, within the first 15 minutes to sing their song 'El Makinon.' Later in the set, Karol G brought out Mexican-American singer (and Coachella regular) Becky G to sing their song "Mamiii," which the pair also sang together during Karol G's Coachella debut in 2022. Then, there was a long section of the set in which Karol G brought out Puerto Rican rapper/singer Wisin — one half of the iconic reggaeton duo Wisin & Yandel — to sing four of the duo's songs.

1:34 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update Karol G begins her headlining set 30 minutes late Shad Powers Karol G, who made history Sunday as the first Latina headliner in Coachella history, didn't start her set until 10:25 p.m., exactly a half hour later than her scheduled start time of 9:55 p.m.Workers were spotted still on the stage setting things up several minutes after the set start time, and multiple crowd members in the front row were chanting “Si se puede” to encourage them.It got people in the audience antsy, and someone was heard saying “she’s on Colombian time.” The curfew for performers on Sunday night is midnight, so it's not clear if Karol G will have to cut some of her planned two-hour show or if she will just go over the time deadline and incur a fine.

1:26 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update Icelandic songstress Laufey could headline Coachella, she's that good Shad Powers Her name doesn't rhyme with toffee or taffy, it's actually pronounced LAY-vay, but the 26-year-old prodigy from Iceland put on such a soulful, confident, creative performance Sunday at the Outdoor Theatre at Coachella, that's it's impossible not to foresee huge things in her future. And that could include a Coachella headlining. Before you say it's crazy, remember that a quarter of all female Coachella headliners have been Icelandic (Bjork twice). Laufey, whose sound is a throwback to an era of jazz and bossa nova, played guitar, piano and cello throughout her hour-long set. She has the charisma, pipes and flair to pull off a cinematic Lady Gaga Coachella 2025-esque show. Her closing message for her fans — and all the creatives watching — on Sunday was particularly powerful. "It doesn't matter what kind of art you make, there's an audience for it," she said. "Stick to what makes you unique. I'm raised on classical and jazz, and I never would've imagined playing a festival like this — yet here we are at Coachella. What an honor." The honor will be ours when she headlines in, let's say, 2029.

12:41 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update FKA twigs transforms Mojave tent into ballroom scene Ema Sasic English songstress FKA twigs turned the Mojave tent into a pulse-pounding New York City ballroom club, captivating and impressing at every turn. The ethereal singer began her set rising from a comfy bed, looking angelic as she sang "meta angel." Her stage design was fairly simple, but there were touches added that made everything pop. A shadow from a dancer with large, white wings was projected behind the singer, adding to the airy vibe, while flashing lights got the audience in the club scene. From then, FKA twigs did not let up with the showmanship. Her voice was astounding, effortlessly hitting the delicate high notes on "Eusexua." But her dancing is truly a wonder to witness. Whether she was in-sync with her dancers for an avant-garde piece or showing off her flexibility, you can't help but be in awe of all that she does. Even when FKA twigs was off the stage for a costume change — which there were multiple, like a matching white tank top and underwear and a black gothic gown with red spikes sticking up from the back — her impressive troupe of dancers kept the party going. Once they broke out into a ballroom scene, it was madness inside the Mojave, with the crowd jumping and screaming at all the voguing and death drops.

12:36 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update Iggy Pop thrills Mojave crowd Brian Blueskye Iggy Pop brought “Raw Power” to the Mojave on Sunday night, and the 78‑year‑old icon showed up with an impressive live band behind him, which included Yeah Yeah Yeahs' guitarist Nick Zinner, Interpol bassist Brad Truax, The Armed’s Urian Hackney on drums, and Joan Wasser (Joan As Police Woman) on keys. The set was packed with highlights: a full‑throated crowd sing-along during “The Passenger,” “I Wanna Be Your Dog,” “Gimme Danger,” “Lust for Life,” and plenty more. Even as his scoliosis has become more visible in recent years, he’s still performing shirtless, still prowling the stage, and still delivering the kind of raw, high‑energy rock show that only Iggy Pop can.

12:35 am ET April 13, 2026 Share this update Black Flag set shows skill but lacks spirit Brian Blueskye The Los Angeles hardcore punk band Black Flag has endured many lineup changes since forming in 1976, featuring different eras under frontmen such as Henry Rollins, Keith Morris and Ron Reyes. But in 2025, guitarist Greg Ginn announced another reformation featuring significantly younger musicians from the area near where he now lives in Texas. That was the lineup that performed on Sunday in the Gobi tent during the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival. When the band opened with "Can't Decide" followed by the classic "Nervous Breakdown," watching the 71-year-old Greg Ginn perform alongside vocalist Max Zanelly, bassist David Rodriguez, and drummer Bryce Weston felt nothing like Black Flag. However, the musicianship of Ginn and the rest of the band was exceptional. Attendance at the Gobi was decent, but it consisted of curious onlookers with skepticism, those indifferent who moshed or screamed along to the lyrics of “Six Pack” and “My War” for the hell of it, spectators who peeked in and left, some who wanted to give it a chance, and others who walked away in disgust.

11:18 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Young Thug set features Ty Dolla $ign, Camila Cabello, Nav, drone show Shad Powers Rapper Young Thug kicked off his Sunday night main stage set with a drone show that started out as a huge red sphere, then devolved into a sparkly cloud. Then, when the music started, the drones popped to life again in a static version of his YSL logo ("Young Stoner Life," not "Yves Saint Laurent"), which stayed there through the first three songs and then sprinkled away. Young Thug was joined on stage by Ty Dolla $ign to start the show with "Carnival," and four songs in, Camila Cabello popped up to sing her megahit "Havana." The special guests continued when Young Thug brought out Canadian rapper Nav to perform the live debut of their song "Trimski."

11:15 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Foster the People channels Palm Springs mid-mod Ema Sasic Indie rock band Foster the People returned to Coachella after 12 years on Sunday, and it was clear the crowd missed them dearly. From the first notes of their opener, "Helena Beat," one of their many throwback songs throughout the set, viewers were locked into their set and singing along to all the words. While I admit I haven't kept up with the band much since their breakout in the early 2010s, others in the crowd have followed this group through thick and thin. What really stood out to this Palm Springs resident wasn't frontman Mark Foster's pitch-perfect voice, but rather their mid-century modern backdrop. A home that could very easily make it onto Modernism Week's feature home tours was behind them, featuring a butterfly roof and plenty of color. A 1960s "nuclear family" also appeared on screen a number of times throughout the set. Right before the group went into their explosive hit, "Pumped Up Kicks," Foster spoke directly to the many artists in the crowd, both those who have found success and those still trying to find their footing. About a year and a half before the group made its Coachella debut in 2012, he said he was considering quitting music after years of being a struggling musician. But then everything changed once "Pumped Up Kicks" came out. In dark times, Foster said that people turn to artists for comfort, and he encouraged people to continue sharing their point of view and voice.

10:36 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Suicidal Tendencies band brings out Thundercat Niki Kottmann Los Angeles heavy metal group Suicidal Tendencies rocked the worlds of those in their Mojave tent audience early Sunday evening when they brought out fellow Angeleno Thundercat, aka singer/producer/bassist Stephen Lee Bruner, to perform with them.

10:30 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Major Lazer Coachella 2026 set features M.I.A. Niki Kottmann Major Lazer, aka the electronic music project of record producer Diplo, DJs Walshy Fire and Ape Drums and vocalist America Foster, tore up the Coachella Stage late Sunday afternoon and brought out British singer/rapper M.I.A. to sing "Paper Planes."

9:16 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Anyma, Isaiah Rashad to play surprise Do Lab set on Sunday Ema Sasic After Anyma's much-hyped new ÆDEN show was canceled on Friday, April 10, many festivalgoers were bummed to miss out on the DJ. Well there's a silver lining in their woes: the Do Lab announced Anyma will hold a surprise set in the electronic dance area. The performance will begin at 10:45 p.m. Sunday. Fans should not hold their breath for an ÆDEN performance during the Do Lab set, however, as the DJ announced on Saturday that there was not a slot available to do the set. They will have to tune into Weekend 2's livestream instead. Marlon Hoffstadt will also perform in the Do Lab later this evening, and Isaiah Rashad was also added to the lineup, and is performing as we speak. Anyma was originally scheduled to debut his new show on the main Coachella Stage Friday night, only for the festival to make a last-minute decision to cancel the show because of winds in the area and concerns for audience safety.

8:40 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Clipse brings out special guest Travis Barker Niki Kottmann Clipse, the Grammy Award-winning rap duo comprised of brothers Pusha T and Malice, brought out Blink-182 drummer Travis Barker for their Sunday afternoon set at the Outdoor Theatre.

8:38 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Wet Leg Coachella 2026 set features horsegiirL Niki Kottmann English Indie rock band Wet Leg brought a familiar face — sort of — onstage during their Coachella main stage debut Sunday. The DJ/singer horsegiirL, who also appeared during PinkPantheress' Saturday Coachella set, came out to jam with the girls in her signature mask.

7:46 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update The Chats perform wild early afternoon show in the Gobi tent Brian Blueskye An afternoon time slot — especially 3 p.m. — on the Sunday of any Coachella weekend is typically considered early, but fans who arrived to see the Australian punk band The Chats were eager to party. From start to finish, their performance was wild. The band's songs are rowdy and brash, leaving no room for subtlety. Frontman Eamon Zambia Sandwith asked the crowd to scream a tawdry message in unison with him for those livestreaming at home, which many did and some with their middle fingers in the air. Fans were also moshing throughout the show.

7:23 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update What time is BIGBANG performing at Coachella? Niki Kottmann Beloved K-pop group BIGBANG will reunite for the first time since 2020 at Coachella 2026, and their Sunday set will be streamed at 10:30 p.m. PDT on the official YouTube Coachella livestream.

6:36 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Did it just rain at Coachella? Yes, but it stopped before I could finish writing this Paul Albani-Burgio and Shad Powers The skies darkened toward the west Sunday afternoon and then briefly delivered something that has not been seen at Coachella in quite some time: rain. At around 3 p.m., a few drops of rain were felt in the press tent. But then, just as quickly as it started, the rain seemed to stop with nary a sign it had happened at all. But while the rain provided a momentary novelty, the bigger weather concerns for the rest of the day were likelier to be wind and relatively cool temperatures that could drop into the mid-50s by the time the festival was wrapping up. The National Weather Service was forecasting winds of 10 to 15 mph and gusts of up to 30 mph for Sunday evening and into Monday, meaning campers could be left to contend windy conditions as they dismantle their sites and head out Monday morning.

5:07 pm ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Karol G headlines on rare chilly April day in Indio as Coachella Day 3 is off to windy start Shad Powers The final day of the first weekend is upon us with a breezy start to a day that will conclude with a history-making performance of Karol G as the first Latina headliner. At 2 p.m., there were 20 mph winds whipping through the polo grounds and a high temperature forecast for 77 degrees. For comparison, the high in Indio on April 12 in 2025 was 100 degrees, in 2024 it was 90 and in 2023 it was 91 degrees. The low temperature Sunday night is slated to be 59 degrees, and if it's still windy, we could be using the phrase "wind-chill factor" at Coachella for the first time at the desert festival.

5:32 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Car erupts in flames in Red Lot as Coachella festivalgoers are trying to leave Andy Abeyta and Niki Kottmann Three Desert Sun journalists were trying to exit Red Lot, the Coachella festival day parking lot next to the campgrounds, early Sunday morning when they noticed smoke coming from behind them. Soon, two firetrucks and several police cars (somehow) squeezed between traffic and got into the lot to take control of a white BMW that was on fire. Two engines arrived, and the fire is now out. No injuries were reported, and the vehicle owner was safe and on site.

2:10 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update The Strokes deliver the best set of Coachella 2026 (so far) Paul Albani-Burgio Were The Strokes too much for Coachella's sound system? That’s the conclusion I came to after their thrilling Coachella show was interrupted by several ever-so-brief instances of the sound system cutting out, only to be restored within a second or two. The issue did nothing to detract from what was a truly thrilling show. On the contrary, it only seemed to emphasize how absolutely rocking it was. My Desert Sun colleagues and I have seen dozens of Coachella sets on the main stage between us, and we all agreed we could not recall another set that came anywhere close to matching the sheer volume with which The Strokes hits (and a few deeper cuts) were being blasted through the speakers. It was the perfect way to see one of the great bands of the early 2000s, who also took advantage of Coachella’s ability to create a spectacle to accompany several of their tunes with a hypnotic barrage of lasers. The classic tune “Someday” which was accompanied not by lasers but by a nostalgic-feeling video of sights from the band’s hometown of New York, was an early highlight of the show. I also cherished hearing The Strokes’ biggest hit from their most recent album, “The Adults are Talking,” which sounded even better live than I would’ve expected. But the true showstopper was, of course, “Reptilia” which saw the band take full advantage of the booming speakers and lasers at their disposal to deliver the best performance of a song I have heard at Coachella this year. Read more

1:33 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Precious Renee Tucker, The Dare, horsegiirL come onstage with PinkPantheress Ema Sasic British singer-songwriter PinkPantheress created a club house vibe inside the Mojave tent Saturday, and she got a few friends to help her out with it too. Among those on her "VIP" list were Precious Renee Tucker, who helped play the piano on "Pain" toward the beginning of PinkPantheress' set. Later on, as the club continued to get more lit, a few more famous faces popped up. The Dare came out to play a remix of Addison Rae's "Fame is a Gun" and Charli XCX's "Guess" while PinkPantheress did a costume change into a new outfit including at top with a huge bow on it. DJ horsegiirL was also spotted on stage as a fake club scene emerged and certain people were being allowed "inside." Actor Tyriq Withers appeared as a special guest a couple times during her set, playing Romeo. The two appeared to end the set with an onstage kiss, although a sign with the words "The End" blocked the audiences' view.

1:30 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update David Guetta Coachella set featured hits to fire up the Quasar crowd Sam Morgen Sometimes keeping it simple is the best option. That was the case for David Guetta at the Quasar Stage Saturday night. The French DJ played a succession of EDM remixes of popular songs, firing up the crowd into an energetic frenzy. What do the songs "Day 'n' Nite" by Kid Cudi, "Satisfaction" by Benny Benassi, "Guess" by Charli XCX and "Club Can't Handle Me" by Flo Rida have in common? The answer is, you guessed it, they were all played in sequences by Guetta during this Coachella set. That was just a sampling of the Top 40 hits mixed into Guetta's roughly two-hour performance. The DJ put on a clinic on hyping up the crowd, exhibiting the talents that have allowed him to amass more than 30 billion streams on Spotify. The 58-year-old said it had been 10 years since he last played at Coachella. It felt like he'd never left.

1:25 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Labrinth Coachella set starts off weird, loses the audience Ema Sasic After Labrinth posted a very cryptic message on social media a few weeks ago — saying he was "done with this industry" — many were wondering what on earth he'd do during his Coachella set. Well, we can now report that he went a little weird. A graphic popped up on the screen saying in order for one to heal, they have to sit with the internal chaos. It was followed by a graphic of the singer free falling. So, clearly off to a great start. For the first 15 minutes of his set, a masked Labrinth was seen inside some type of industrial cage contraption on stage. Honestly, it was hard to make out what was going on, especially because we couldn't actually see his face (for a second I didn't even know if it was him on stage) and the graphics on screen were animated and often not clear. He didn't call out to the audience at all during that time, so it mainly felt like he was going through the motion. Finally, when he removed his mask and slowly started coming out of the cage, the artistic and creative Labrinth took over. His powerful vocals took center stage, and the visuals continued to entice. At one point, he had a camera attached to his microphone, and we all got an up-close-and-personal look at the singer. Perhaps the upside of this performance came a bit too late for festivalgoers, as several were seen continuously leaving the set. Once The Strokes began their performance at the Coachella Stage, however, their speakers drowned out Labrinth's set, leading to even more people exiting.

12:42 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Geese prompts an early Justin Bieber singalong Sam Morgen The Brooklyn-band Geese prompted an early Justin Bieber singalong by covering one of the singer's biggest hits hours before the headliner was set to take the stage. During a sunset performance in the Gobi Theater, Geese rocked out in their idiosyncratic style. About 15 minutes into their set, they broke into Bieber's 2010 hit, "Baby." While the crowd had been swaying appreciatively during the beginning of the set, they started cheering and singing along at the start of "Baby." It might say something about the band that one of the biggest moments of their Coachella set was a song from another artist, but the crowd didn't seem to care. It was a smart choice and it paid off. Maybe next time they'll get the crowd going with one of their own songs.

12:37 am ET April 12, 2026 Share this update Nine Inch Noize Coachella set proves visually stunning, haunting Brian Blueskye The Saturday performance by Nine Inch Noize, a collaboration between Nine Inch Nails and Boys Noize, featured a haunting, surreal, and intense musical and visual production. Before the performance began, a large, tilted triangle with a platform at its center was set up on the Sahara stage. When the music began, the red lighting and blend of industrial and dark electronic music began, featuring a reimagining of Nine Inch Nails’ sound and the two recent collaborations on the “TRON: Ares” and “Challengers” soundtracks. When a group of dancers repeatedly climbed up and slid down the triangular structure that resembled a volcano, a pyramid in an apocalyptic sky, or other various settings, it made the 45-minute performance more stirring. The set included some Nine Inch Nails classics such as “Heresy,” “Closer,” and “Copy of A.” The visual effects and electronic music presentation was a perfect performance for the Sahara tent, but the stage's overwhelming bass featured in DJ sets took away from the vocals.

11:26 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Sombr brings out Billy Corgan, shows rock star side Ema Sasic Whether you went into Sombr's set knowing his songs or were just curious about this Timothée Chalamet/Finn Wolfhard-looking man, chances are you left as a fan. The Grammy-nominated pop rocker had the crowd in the palm of his hand from the start. I mean, when you step out in a black leather jacket and pants and wearing a black lace shirt underneath, who wouldn't want to be in your corner? Sombr looked good and sounded incredible throughout the whole set, pouring his heart out into emotional songs such as "i wish i knew how to quit you" and "potential." And just to show how much the crowd was into him, they obliged to jump for him even when he called all of them "losers" via his video screen. Based on the enormous turnout of festivalgoers of all ages — I even spotted an infant in the crowd — it's clear Sombr is our next rock superstar. But seemingly not wanting to let all the fame go to his head, he brought out legendary Smashing Pumpkins frontman Billy Corgan — making his Coachella debut — to sing the band's hit "1979." Though it was initially plagued with mic issues for Corgan, they bounced back by the time the iconic chorus began. I didn't know too much about your music prior to this set, Sombr, but now, consider me a fan.

11:05 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Royel Otis members rock Mojave during afternoon set Brian Blueskye The Australian indie-pop duo Royel Otis drew a full crowd at the Mojave tent on Saturday afternoon. They opened their performance with a humorous video featuring a pink background that welcomed fans to the desert. In the video, they playfully advised attendees not to check their bank accounts the following day and suggested they could sleep through the next week. It was another rock performance proving that there's an upswing on the genre at the festival by established a newer generation of bands. Geese received the same reception in the nearby Gobi tent, and so did SOMBR on the Outdoor Theatre stage.

9:29 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Sabrina Carpenter apologizes after Coachella ‘confusion’ Paris Barraza Sabrina Carpenter is facing some criticism online due to an interaction she had with an audience member at Coachella. The moment was captured in a clip that was shared in several posts on X as of April 11. In the clip, a sound seemingly coming from the crowd can be heard among more general audience noise. “Is that what you’re doing?” Carpenter asked. A person can be heard responding, followed by Carpenter saying, “I don’t like it.” The exchange continued. At one point, Carpenter referred to the sound as “yodeling,” and the person could be heard trying to explain. Although the audio is at times a bit weak, words like “culture,” “call” and “celebration” can be heard from the person trying to explain to Carpenter. “Is this Burning Man? What’s going on? This is weird,” the Grammy-winner said. Several users on social media responded to the interaction with explanations of the sound and referred to it as a zaghrouta, a cultural vocalization. Carpenter issued an apology in a post on X. “My apologies I didn’t see this person with my eyes and couldn’t hear clearly,” Carpenter’s post said. “My reaction was pure confusion, sarcasm and not ill intended. Could have handled it better! Now I know what a Zaghrouta is! I welcome all cheers and yodels from here on out.”

9:27 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Coachella livestream schedule 2026? See times Paris Barraza Here’s the official livestream schedule from Coachella’s Instagram account. 9:23 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update How long does Coachella last? Paris Barraza The festival runs for two weekends in April. This year, that’s April 10-12 and April 17-19. The entrance to the festival grounds — not parking, but where performances will be held — opens around 1 p.m. “ish,” according to Coachella. On Fridays and Saturdays, venue curfew (aka when the live music must stop, or else Goldenvoice gets fined) is 1 a.m. both Fridays and Saturdays, and on the Sundays, it’s at midnight, said Coachella.

9:19 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Maddie Ziegler joins Addison Rae at Coachella 2026 Paris Barraza Maddie Ziegler, the actress and former “Dance Moms” star, joined Addison Rae on stage during the Tik Tok-turned-pop star's first Coachella Stage set (or, at least, first as a lineup artist rather than a special guest). The dancer, who has starred in music videos for singer Sia, surprised fans during Rae’s performance of “Aquamarine.” And unsurprisingly, she showed off her talent for dancing throughout the brief appearance.

8:18 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Who's playing at Coachella 2026? Paris Barraza Sabrina Carpenter, Justin Bieber and Karol G are headlining the festival this year. 8:17 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Where's Coachella 2026? Paris Barraza Coachella is held at the Empire Polo Club at 81-800 Avenue 51 in Indio, California. Indio, which is part of the Coachella Valley, is more than 100 miles southeast of downtown Los Angeles.

6:54 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Beliebers line up at the Coachella Stage hours before headliner set to perform Sam Morgen Although Justin Bieber isn't set to perform until 11:25 p.m., the superstar's biggest fans started lining up in front of the stage just after the gates opened at 1:20 p.m. Dozens of people sat in their Bieber gear, with parasols, in the 80-degree heat Saturday afternoon. Bieber hasn't performed a large-scale concert since the 2022 Justice World Tour (other than his recent Grammys performance), which was canceled when the artist was diagnosed with Ramsay Hunt Syndrome, a neurological symptom that can paralyze the face. The headlining set on Saturday has been billed as Bieber's long-awaited comeback, and the fans are eager to see it. Trinity James and Leah Vanderpool traveled to Indio from Ohio to view the performance. They said they had been fans since they were 13 years old and had promised themselves they would attend Coachella if he headlined. "As long as we see him, that is all we need," James, 25, said. "He could stand there and say nothing and that would be OK." The pair have been to four of Beiber's concerts already and said they hoped he would play some old hits, but would be satisfied if he stuck with his newer music. But they have a few more hours to wait before they find out.

6:42 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Coachella DJ set canceled, Do Lab back up and running Niki Kottmann "Dustchella" caused several issues Friday night, first when a light — likely aided by the wind — supposedly fell during DJ John Summit's surprise set in the Do Lab and hit an attendee. According to several festivalgoers' social media posts, the attendee was injured, and the incident caused organizers to temporarily close the Do Lab. Festival promoter Goldenvoice has not provided an official statement about the incident, and we're waiting for a response to our questions. Later in the night, another DJ set was impacted by the wind. Anyma, the melodic techno project of Italian-American DJ/producer Matteo Milleri, canceled his Friday night closing set on the Coachella Stage, citing wind and concerns for audience safety. The decision, which the official Coachella Instagram account posted about, was made by both the festival and the artist, and was likely linked to the earlier incident in the Do Lab. However, when we were back on the festival grounds around 2 p.m. Saturday, the Do Lab was fully back up and running, with speakers blasting music by producer/ DJ duo Baalti.

6:22 pm ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Strong winds kick up 'Dustchella' conditions across Coachella Valley James Ward Gusty winds and blowing dust are turning Coachella 2026 Weekend 1 into familiar “Dustchella” conditions across the Coachella Valley as a windblown dust advisory remains in effect. Forecasters warn that winds strengthening later in the day could kick up blowing dust, reduce visibility and worsen air quality — especially during evening and overnight hours when festival crowds are at their largest. Air quality levels have reached “unhealthy for sensitive groups,” and even healthy adults may feel irritation in their eyes, throat and lungs during dusty stretches. Officials urge attendees to take precautions and plan around peak wind periods. The festival will go on, but conditions are shaping up to be a classic Dustchella scenario, particularly at night. Read the whole story: 'Dustchella' conditions hit Coachella as wind, dust advisory issued

12:00 am ET April 11, 2026 Share this update Turnstile Coachella set turns Outdoor Theatre into mosh pit Brian Blueskye The beginning of Turnstile’s set at the Outdoor Theatre kicked off with an intro video featuring a fan stating, “It’s like we want to kill each other, but we’re all friends.” This encapsulates the intense energy the hardcore band brings to every performance, including their set at Coachella on Friday. 10:30 pm ET April 10, 2026 Share this update The Two Lips make the crowd swoon Sam Morgen The crowds during Coachella can be notoriously difficult. 9:31 pm ET April 10, 2026 Share this update Bini Coachella debut begs the question, are they the next Blackpink? Paul Albani-Burgio Could BINI do for the Philippines what another girl group like Blackpink once did for K pop? That's what I was thinking about as I walked out of the Mojave after the group’s energetic 50-minute set. I walked into the tent midway through what I think was the first song – not knowing anything about BINI – and quickly deduced that I might’ve been the only person to not be in the know (yes, it stinks getting old). An enthusiastic crowd was already spilling beyond the bounds of the Mojave, many of whom were singing along to the band’s mostly Filipino lyrics. Now, I can’t say that I was overly impressed by BINI. But I wasn’t underwhelmed either. The songs were catchy with infectious beats, the girls’ vocal talents were serviceable and the show’s visuals was heavy on bright, candy-like colors that felt perfectly matched to the sundrenched Coachella afternoon. One way I thought they fell a bit short was their dancing (at times I felt those dancing around me in the crowd outshined what was happening on stage) but these girls radiated enough beauty and rizz to more than hold the stage. But who cares what I think? The crowd was eating it up, never more than when they roared as the launched into an extended version of their closing song “Pantropiko.” And BINI clearly recognized the magnitude of the moment and what it could mean for their careers. Early in the set, one of the singers explained that “we are so grateful to be here to represent the Philippines on the global stage.” They also are one of the few bands I have ever seen to speak directly to the audience watching on the live screen. Read more

7:42 pm ET April 10, 2026 Share this update Walk down Coachella memory lane at Bella + Canvas pop-up Brian Blueskye Did you attend Coachella in the past and have a t-shirt (or several) that you still wear? 7:38 pm ET April 10, 2026 Share this update Slayyyter Coachella debut one of the best of fest thus far Brian Blueskye There’s a billboard on Interstate 10 going eastbound in Indio advertising that Teddy Swims’ Friday afternoon set “will make you lose control,” only in less flattering terms when describing him. But earlier in the afternoon, that’s exactly what happened for those who watched Slayyyter’s set in the Mojave. The Missouri-born singer and songwriter filled the tent, and there was overflow in the back and on the sides as fans began chanting her name moments before she came out on stage. She performed backed by a rock band, with a clothesline of t-shirts and a rug hanging off it, a merry-go-round horse off to the side, and a video-production package of assorted visuals. At times, it matched the energy of last year’s Charli XCX set, and the songs blended rock, pop, metal, electronic, and dance music. Two songs that got the biggest responses were the songs “Old Technology” which featured the lyrics “I’m doing drugs tonight” flashing on the video screen, and “$T. Loser.” The 45-minute set didn’t include any special guests and closed with “BEAT UP CHANEL$.” With the heavy attendance, crowd reception and dominating stage performance of Slayyyter, it’s likely we’ll see her again at Coachella in the future and much higher on the lineup.

6:05 pm ET April 10, 2026 Share this update 'I can't wait to see Justin Bieber' Febuary screamo singer declares in early set Paul Albani-Burgio If you were up late playing one too many games of beer pong at your campsite last night, Las Vegas screamo outfit Febuary’s early afternoon set was exactly what the doctor ordered to wake you up and make you feel alive again. Of course, it helped that there was plenty of screaming. But there was also the genuine glee lead singer Rila Ogawa seemed to feel at being able to attend her first Coachella as part of a band playing in the event. “I never thought I would get to go to Coachella,” she explained early in the set. “It’s expensive and I’m broke.” A few minutes later, Ogawa announced the artist that she was most looking forward to. “I can’t wait to see Justin Bieber,” she said, a statement that proved that even screamo girls can love the Biebs. “I hope he plays 'One Less Lonely Girl,' that would be awesome.” But it was the thoughts she shared before the last song, which she explained was about her struggles as a woman existing in the male-dominated screamo space, that hit this grizzled Coachella veteran right in the gut. After calling on those in the crowd to speak up about what she called “the horrible things in the world” and explaining that the band members come from immigrant families, Ogawa took a final moment to take stock of the moment. “Mom, I did it,” she declared. Yes girl, you sure did.

2:23 am ET April 10, 2026 Share this update Take a peek at Sabrina Carpenter's 'pit stop' in Indio Paris Barraza Sabrina Carpenter’s “pit stop” is about to open for business in Indio, offering visitors fruity slushies, access to merchandise and, most importantly, vibes all weekend. “Sabrina’s Pit Stop,” announced by Airbnb earlier this month, coincides with her Coachella headliner debut. Weekend 1 of Coachella 2026 kicks off on Friday, April 10 — as does Carpenter’s pit stop in Indio. The USA TODAY Network visited the fan experience on Thursday, April 9, which Carpenter called a “surrealist fantasy land” of hers in a statement. "Sabrina's Pit Stop" is as if your local gas station was suddenly featured in a music video for the pop singer, and you get to show up to take the perfect selfie. We’re talking bubblegum pink, sky blue and red vintage cars stationed around gas pumps labeled “Sabrina’s Pit Stop.” And those who’ve seen Carpenter’s “House Tour” music video will recognize one of the vehicles on site. Also, be sure to peek into the truck of the vehicles, which house merch like shirts and hats. "Sabrina's Pit Stop" also has refreshments, a.k.a. slushies, inside an intimate space cast in blue and red lighting with a grand chandelier. Slushie flavors are mango, coconut pineapple, cherry, root beer and blue raspberry. Be sure to listen as you explore, because Carpenter curated the music playing, an Airbnb spokesperson said. 7:16 pm ET April 9, 2026 Share this update How many days is Coachella? Kate Franco The Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival in Indio, California is staged in two, three-day installments. Coachella 2026 Weekend 1 is Friday, April 10 through Sunday April 12 and Coachella 2026 Weekend 2 is Friday, April 17 through Sunday, April 19. While the lineups are basically the same, the festival organizers generally have some surprises that are unveiled when set times are published. For Weekend 1 this year, for example, Jack White was revealed as a surprised act on Saturday afternoon. Set times between the two weekends are generally not identical.

7:10 pm ET April 9, 2026 Share this update What is the Coachella bag policy? Niki Kottmann Coachella allows bags that are a "medium-sized backpack" or smaller. So, what does that actually mean? The festival phrases it on the website like this: "A medium backpack should be no bigger than 18”x13”x8.5” when fully packed. Bags will have to fit into a box of that size, similar to the system used to check the size of carry-on luggage at airports. If the bag doesn’t fit in the box, you won’t be allowed inside the Venue." Essentially, any regular-sized backpack you would have used back in your high school or college days will work perfectly. Some people don't want to carry around a bag that big, however, so we highly recommend either bringing a smaller cross-body bag — it doesn't need to be clear — that you carry your essentials in, or bringing a regular backpack to put bulkier items in, along with your smaller bag inside, and then you can get a locker to put the bigger bag/bulkier items in and just carry around the smaller bag during the day. Still have other Coachella questions? Check this out.

5:01 pm ET April 9, 2026 Share this update Small group gathers outside Coachella 2026 to protest ICE Ema Sasic As Coachella campers started making their way toward the Empire Polo Club grounds on Thursday, April 9, a small group of ICE protesters gathered nearby. Three protestors were spotted around 9:30 a.m. at the intersection of 50th Avenue and Madison Street in Indio, right by one of the festival's day parking lots. One of their signs read "ampm FUELS ICE" and encouraged drivers to boycott gas stations Arco and BP. Another sign read "Latina Foreva," a reference to headliner Karol G's song of the same name, and "ICE never." Drivers were spotted giving the group thumbs up as they passed by. A Facebook post shared by one of the protest organizers said they would hold additional protests from 2 to 5 p.m. Sunday, April 12 and 19 at the corner of Avenue 49 and Monroe Street, as well as 8 to 11 a.m. Thursday, April 16, at 50th Avenue and Madison Street.

Going to Coachella Weekend 2? 6 tips to make it better than ...
search_result.text:
April 16, 2019 Updated April 17, 2019, 8:45 p.m. ET

We came with our flower crowns, we saw Janelle Monáe tear up the main stage, we conquered the Indio Central Market by finding the secret disco bar.

That's a wrap for Weekend 1 of the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival.

But we also made some mistakes — though none were as bad as the repeated flubs from the sound board team during Lizzo's Sunday set. To make our — and your — Weekend 2 experience that much better, here's what we learned and what we'll do better.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

1. If you want to see Idris Elba, get there early.

The DJ's Saturday evening set in the Yuma tent was packed to capacity. Festival security denied access to a Desert Sun photographer 15 minutes before Elba's Weekend 1 performance, citing fire safety hazards. If you want to see the actor spin beats, give yourself plenty of time to get in the venue.

2. The Outdoor Theatre is where it's at.

The venue is maybe even better than the main Coachella stage. We're talking Ella Mai, DJ Snake, Billie Eilish, BASSNECTAR and H.E.R. Despite delays throughout Weekend 1, the performances there over the first three days of Coachella were epic.

The crowd for Billie Eilish was one of the largest in recent memory at the Outdoor Theatre. DJ Snake offered an instant-classic moment when he brought out Selena Gomez, Cardi B and Ozuna during his set. And H.E.R. showed festivalgoers exactly why she won those two Grammys this year in a Sunday night set.

3. "MISMO" by Sofia Enriquez and "H.i.P.O." by Dedo Vabo aren't just art installations.

They're live performance art! Everyday from 4 to 7 p.m., a fashion show featuring Sofia Enriquez's custom clothing takes place at "MISMO." (She hand-paints on vintage items for super funky capes, shoes and more.) And the super absurd "H.i.P.O." features nearly 200 performers in hippo costumes for 12 hours a day — yeah, those animals in the installation are actually real humans.

4. The Heineken House is the place to be on Saturday night.

De La Soul and The Roots took the Heineken House by storm Saturday night, treating a diverse and boisterous crowd to a show packed with infectious energy and incredible musicianship. The energetic crowd couldn't get enough of the bands, including several crews who put on the sickest break-dancing clinic — pop-and-lock anyone? — delivered since 1983. Get there if you can.

5. Want to see Billie Eilish? Good luck.

You're going to need to get there earlier than Idris Elba — like, you should probably head over to the Outdoor Theatre on Thursday. Despite starting her Coachella set almost 40 minutes late on Saturday, causing hundreds of upset fans to leave, the crowd was one of the largest in recent memory at the festival's Outdoor Theatre.

6. There's a secret bar — and you don't have to be a VIP.

While Coachella fans may be familiar with the festival's secret VIP tiki bar, there's a new disco-themed hangout, and all you need is a general admission wristband to get in. Hush Hush is a disco-themed hangout reminiscent of New York City's Studio 54. Tucked next to an undisclosed food stand, it has disco balls, mirrors and a long bar capable of serving multiple patrons. The bar is open from 1 p.m. to midnight and sells mixed drinks like "Penichillin," "Sex On The Beach," "Mezcal Sunrise" and "Blue Hawaiian." At 3 p.m., there was a 25 minute wait during Weekend 1. Head there later, for the New Year's Eve ball drop that happens at 9 p.m. every night.

Kristin Scharkey is the editor of DESERT magazine and features editor at The Desert Sun. Reach her at kristin.scharkey@desertsun.com or on Twitter @kscharkey. Desert Sun reporter Shane Newell contributed to this report.

search_result.title: Coachella, Stagecoach music festivals postponed amid coronavirus ...
search_result.text:
March 10, 2020 Updated March 21, 2020, 12:09 a.m. ET

Goldenvoice, the Los Angeles-based company that produces the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival and its country music counterpart Stagecoach over three weekends in April, has announced the postponement of both festivals due to growing public health concerns about the rapidly spreading coronavirus.

Coachella is being rescheduled for Oct. 9-11 and 16-18. Stagecoach will take place Oct. 23-25. The decision to delay the events comes after the cancellation of the BNP Paribas Open tennis tournament in Indian Wells and the declaration of a public health emergency in Riverside County by the top public health officer on Sunday, March 8.

"At the direction of the County of Riverside and local health authorities, we must sadly confirm the rescheduling of Coachella and Stagecoach due to COVID-19 concerns," Goldenvoice said in an email Tuesday. "While this decision comes at a time of universal uncertainty, we take the safety and health of our guests, staff and community very seriously. We urge everyone to follow the guidelines and protocols put forth by public health officials."

Coachella postponed: Six reasons to get excited for an October festival

Riverside County Public Health Officer Dr. Cameron Kaiser ordered the cancellation of both April festivals, citing "concerns about the possible health risks because of the ongoing move coronavirus," according to an email from spokeswoman Brooke Frederico.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

“This decision was not taken lightly or without consideration of many factors,” Kaiser said in a prepared statement. “No doubt it will impact many people, but my top priority is to protect the health of the entire community.”

Kaiser said the decision to cancel was "made to prevent the spread of the illness that has sickened hundreds of thousands worldwide and killed thousands."

A total of six Riverside County residents have tested positive for COVID-19, otherwise known as coronavirus, as of Monday afternoon. Two people are being treated outside of the county, two are being treated at hospitals in the Coachella Valley, and two are quarantined at home in the Coachella Valley.

Several other conferences and large-scale events, including Ultra Music Festival in Miami and South by Southwest music, film and technology conference in Austin, have been canceled.

Coachella was slated to be held over two weekends — April 10-12 and April 17-19. Stagecoach dates were April 24-26.

Travis Scott, Frank Ocean and Rage Against the Machine were to headline Coachella. The Stagecoach lineup included Thomas Rhett, Carrie Underwood, Eric Church, ​​Lil Nas X, Billy Ray Cyrus, ZZ Top and Alan Jackson. Goldenvoice did not address changes to the lineup in its email.

Goldenvoice had already released this year's set times for Stagecoach.

Alice Walker, a California-based singer-songwriter, was scheduled to perform on the Nikki Lane stage at Stagecoach. She also has a West Coast tour planned in April and a summer tour that is now up in the air.

“I’ve spent the last day trying to figure out what my summer is going to look like if this happens, and Stagecoach is probably the biggest anchor date I have out there," Walker said by phone Tuesday morning. "... I might have to completely reschedule my year if everyone else follows suit from the big festivals."

Local artist Alf Alpha, who has performed at Coachella since 2010, said that he respects the decision of Goldenvoice to postpone the festival to October.

“My father is 70-year old full-time resident of the Coachella Valley and his health and the rest of the health of our community, both young and old, have always been a top priority, along with all of our guests that attend the festivals," he said. "As an artist, I honor and respect this decision and believe it’s the most responsible decision. We look forward to putting on a great performance and welcoming music lovers from around the world to our community and home in the Coachella Valley."

What does it mean for desert's other events?

Federico said there are currently no blanket policies or guidelines that dictate when events should be canceled.

Federico said Coachella and Stagecoach were canceled because there is a community spread case in the Coachella Valley, which requires proper social distancing, or 6 to 8 feet between each other. The music festivals are not able to provide the necessary distance between two people to prevent spread.

Additionally, attendees are coming from all over the country and the globe. Federico said this was one of the major concerns that led to the mandate to reschedule.

The timeline of the events was also a concern as the music festivals were coming up quickly and the spread of the virus doesn’t appear to be slowing down. While White Party Palm Springs is also during the same time as the music festival, Federico said the county has yet to determine if that event — billed as the largest gay circuit party in the United States — should be postponed or canceled.

White Party officials said Monday they are closely monitoring the spread of the virus and are following measures that the health department has outlined to protect partygoers, but had no plans to cancel the events.

Federico said the health department is currently working on additional guidelines for other upcoming events to help determine what may or may not be canceled in the near future.

Indio's Mayor Glenn Miller said in a statement that while the annual festivals introduce visitors to the beauty and amenities of the Coachella Valley, "the potential health risks of holding a gathering of a large size must be considered, with the safety of our community being top of mind.”

Goldenvoice approached the city to discuss the possibility of postponing the festival, and they also touched base with local tourism officials on Monday before making the announcement on Tuesday.

Coachella returns to October roots

Goldenvoice is no stranger to hosting festivals at the Empire Polo Club in October. The company has a contract for five festivals per year with the city of Indio. It staged Desert Trip and Phish Fest in October, which is an important month because of weather — plus, it doesn't conflict with polo season, which runs from January through March.

Additionally, Coachella's inaugural event took place Oct. 9-10, 1999, and featured Rage Against the Machine as a headliner.

October is generally a slightly hotter month in the Coachella Valley than April. In April, the average high is 87 degrees and average low is 60, according to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. In October, the average high is 92 and average low is 63.

Organizers said ticketholders will be notified by Friday, March 15, on how to obtain a refund if they are unable to attend the later dates. All purchases for the April dates will be honored in October, according to Goldenvoice.

Music festivals impact valley economy

The music festivals have brought tens of thousands of people to the California desert each spring, filling hotels and boosting the local economy. Combined, those events are believed to generate at least $400 million in local economic impact.

In 2019, the city of Indio received about $3 million from the festival, around two-thirds of which was from ticket surcharges and another $1 million from associated sales tax revenues and transient occupancy taxes from campers.

The 2017 Coachella festival drew about 250,000 visitors to both weekends, plus another 75,000 people for the following week’s Stagecoach Country Music Festival. The combined 2017 regional economic impact exceeded $403 million, according to the 2017 report from the Greater Palm Springs Convention and Visitors Bureau.

Scott White, CEO of the Greater Palm Springs Convention and Visitors Bureau, said that he is thrilled to see the dates postponed instead of canceled outright. While the tourism dollars that typically flow heavily into the valley during March and April — the highest-generating revenue months of the year — will take a hit, more will flow in October.

“That is an Olympic feat, to take a concert with that many variable dynamics to it, and pull it together and say ‘We’re going to hold this in October,’” he said. “It’s going to have a positive impact.”

He pointed to Goldenvoice’s Desert Trip festival in 2016 and said hotel rates averaged $188 a night that year, compared to $134 the year before.

In the meantime, White’s organization is planning to push about $225,000 into extra marketing to promote Palm Springs as a driveable vacation destination to those in Southern California, Arizona and Nevada. With the Easter holiday and spring break coming up, people may be looking to travel closer to home if they cancel flights.

As to whether the postponement could have long-term implications for festival popularity, White said the ubiquity of coronavirus-related cancellations is likely to spare the desert and its marquee events from any stigma.

“Austin had it, Miami had it," White said. "These decisions are being made all over the country and all over the world to contain the virus and limit its spread.”

In Palm Springs, the city that makes the most money from hotel bed taxes, or transient occupancy taxes, April is routinely the highest month for collections. The city made nearly $5.4 million from hotel taxes in April 2019, which amounted to about 15% of the fiscal year’s total collection.

Mayor Geoff Kors couldn’t say how big the hit to those collections might be from the lack of festivals this April. But he said the city isn’t likely to have any cash flow issues; the latest comprehensive annual financial report from the city showed that the city has nearly $40 million in unassigned reserves.

But Kors is concerned about the postponement affecting several thousand workers around the valley who work the festivals and routinely rely on the temp jobs as part of their income stream.

And he’s encouraging local residents to support local businesses like restaurants who rely on festival season income to get them through leaner summer months.

“It’s important for those of us who are not in high-risk categories to continue to go to restaurants and retail and support local businesses,” Kors said.

search_result.title: Reality of Las Vegas shooting: Festival security is not guaranteed to ...
search_result.text:
Sherry Barkas, Benjamin Goad and Wyatt Buchanan

USA TODAY NETWORK

Oct. 4, 2017, 9:00 p.m. ET

PALM SPRINGS, Calif. — In a world of copycat killers, music festival organizers and public officials must grapple with the possibility that America’s major entertainment venues may, in moments, be transformed into killing grounds.

Although authorities from Coachella to Bonnaroo have taken precautions and devised elaborate contingency plans, the massacre at the Route 91 Harvest festival in Las Vegas underscored a devastating reality: No amount of planning and preparation is guaranteed to stop every lone wolf.

Chris Robinette, president of Los Angeles-based Prevent Advisors, which specializes in arena and stadium security, offered a sobering assessment of the Las Vegas tragedy, which left 59 dead and more than 580 wounded.

“I don’t think a lot could have been done to prevent this,” Robinette said, given that gunman Stephen Paddock didn’t appear to have any kind of criminal or mental health history that would have suggested to authorities that he should be watched.

“This was a vicious and nightmarish black swan event,” Robinette said.

Las Vegas shooting: Click here for complete coverage from the USA TODAY NETWORK

After the Manchester, England, terrorist attack at an Ariana Grande concert, entertainment events company Live Nation — promoter of the Route 91 Harvest festival — announced that it was stepping up security efforts, including banning backpacks and bags, at all its venues. But such measures proved futile as the gunman shot at the crowd from his hotel room on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino.

“We are heartbroken over the tragedy that took place at the Route 91 Harvest festival,” the Beverly Hills company said in a statement. “To think that anyone would want to inflict harm on a gathering of music lovers is beyond our comprehension. And while we are stunned and grieving over this incomprehensible act of violence, we know that this is a moment when we must come together to prevent more tragedies like this from occurring.”

Live Nation said its upcoming shows would go on.

Meanwhile, with every tragedy, concert planners around the country continue to evaluate and re-evaluate their venue safety plans.

The Nashville Convention and Visitors Corp. has re-examined safety protocols for the outdoor events and concerts it operates following deadly incidents at concerts in recent years.

After a motorist drove into a crowd at the SXSW festival in Austin, killing four and injuring dozens, the CVC and Nashville police altered the security barriers on the perimeter of the downtown July 4 and New Year’s Eve parties. Typically held in Nashville’s honky tonk district on lower Broadway, the July 4 celebration features live music and fireworks and attracted 240,000 people in 2017.

The CVC moved its New Year’s Eve bash to a nearby public park instead of on lower Broadway, which is surrounded by historic buildings that house bars and restaurants.

Since the SXSW festival, Nashville has used a combination of hydraulic barriers that can open and close, stationary barriers and strategically arranged police cars on side streets that would prevent a runaway vehicle, but allow access to fire trucks and ambulances if necessary.

Following the shooting in Las Vegas, Nashville CVC President and CEO Butch Spyridon said he was communicating with the Nashville police and mayor’s office about how to restrict who has access to buildings that surround the crowd during those large-scale gatherings.

Spyridon said the city also wants to be sure the state law that allows bar owners to ban firearms is properly enforced so that a gunman could not gain access to a building perched above the throngs below.

The CVC also co-promotes the annual CMA Fest along with the Country Music Association. That event draws upwards of 80,000 people to the streets of downtown Nashville.

“Anything outside poses a much greater opportunity for an event of this magnitude,” Spyridon said. “So we’re always looking at how we can improve, what can be done better.”

Outside Nashville at Bonnaroo, organizers have implemented strict security safeguards for years, including searching vehicles that drive onto the rural Tennessee farm that hosts the annual music festival, mandatory metal detectors at each entrance to the festival grounds and armed security, including local police, throughout the property.

In Phoenix, where later this month the inaugural three-day Lost Lake Festival is expected to draw tens of thousands to the 75-acre Steele Indian School Park north of downtown, law enforcement officials say they do not anticipate any major security changes.

“We always work with event organizers to ensure city emergency services are available as part of their overall security plans,” Phoenix Police Sgt. Jonathan Howard said in a statement.

Barnett English, promoter of the global-themed Joshua Tree Music Festival in the Southern California desert, said his festival's health and safety protocol will remain unchanged. The festival, running Friday through Sunday, will have a support crew of 200 people for an expected crowd of 2,500 people, and English said the staff will have “a heightened sensitivity to the feelings of all those around us at the fest.”

The inaugural Desert Oasis Music Festival, running Saturday and Sunday at Empire Polo Club in California, announced on Facebook and Twitter that it will run as scheduled with 24 bands, led by Damian Marley, Rebelution, Steel Pulse, Los Lobos and Kinky. Producer Alex Haagen IV has said the festival expects to attract 10,000 people.

As for the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival, which attracts over 100,000 visitors each weekend to Indio, Calif., event organizer Goldenvoice is required to have a safety operation plan that includes medical, police and fire services. The city of Indio coordinates police, contracted security, medical services and outside law enforcement, but Goldenvoice is required to reimburse it for any out-of-pocket costs. Private security guards patrol nearby neighborhoods and campgrounds and the city has a concert hotline set up that locals can call with questions or concerns.

Indio police Sgt. Dan Marshall and Community Services Supervisor Jim Curtis have said they couldn’t disclose the details of their safety planning, but Marshall said there are contingency plans for everything from a natural disaster to a mass shooting.

“We have staging areas throughout the city; we have plans to get people out as fast as possible; we have plans to shelter people in place,” he said. “We’re definitely not a soft target like somebody might think.”

Robinette applauded efforts of first-responders in their handling of what happened in Vegas, but now all involved need to have more discussions of where any needed improvements can be made, including evacuating people.

“It’s now incumbent upon the professionals to analyze and evaluate and put measures in place” that would prevent a similar incident to occur, Robinette said.

One of the factors organizers and security professionals may have to consider is whether festivals and concerts in urban settings, like Las Vegas or Nashville, pose different—and more complicated—problems than those in more isolated outdoor venues like Coachella or Joshua Tree.

As the Las Vegas shooting suggests, urban settings provide attackers far more vantage points from which to launch their deadly assaults. Like modern urban warfare, contemporary urban concert security may have become a strategic nightmare.

With all this in mind, does Robinette expect Sunday’s massacre to keep people from concerts or sporting events?

“If you look at what happened in the past, what happened on 9/11 and at the Boston Marathon (bombing), we see that Americans are very resilient and defiant,” he said. “I don’t see fear reducing the number of concerts or where they take place.”

Still, Robinette said, concertgoers have a “reasonable expectation that promoters, law enforcement and other first-responders will be looking at this incident for areas and ways they can improve security measures to better ensure their safety."

He added: “The public wants to know how we have learned and made appropriate adjustments."

Follow The (Palm Springs, Calif.) Desert Sun on Twitter: @MyDesert

search_result.title: Lukas Nelson launches month of music festivals in April 2019
search_result.text:
Oct. 3, 2018 Updated Oct. 4, 2018, 2:10 p.m. ET

A two-day music festival launching “Coachella month” 2019 at the Indian Wells Tennis Garden will be headlined by Lukas Nelson and the Promise of the Real, and blues legend Buddy Guy.

But it will have a different name for the third straight year and this time, the city of Indian Wells won’t be a title sponsor.

Wendy Jane Productions, led by desert DJ and McCallum Theatre promoter Jimi Fitz Fitzgerald, will produce the Garden Jam Music Festival in partnership with the Indian Wells Tennis Garden. Fisherman’s Market & Grill is credited as presenting sponsor.

The event will be held April 5-6, one weekend before the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival begins a wild two weeks of music at the Empire Polo Club in Indio. That event is supplemented by “Localchella” shows throughout Southern California and followed by Stagecoach: California’s Country Music Festival in Indio, and the White Party, North America’s largest gay circuit party, in Palm Springs. The Club Skirts Dinah Shore Weekend will draw still more world-class music to the Coachella Valley the same weekend as the Garden Jam Music Festival — making April one full music month.

More: Highlights of 2018 Indian Wells music fest

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

More: Lukas Nelson among 2018 Stagecoach highlights

Last year’s event produced by Fitzgerald and his wife, Wendy Jane, was called the Indian Wells Music Festival because the city of Indian Wells paid $60,000 as title sponsor. Fitzgerald said the city didn’t want to pay that much this year, but still wants to be involved.

The previous Wendy Jane production at the Indian Wells Tennis Garden was the Desert Lexus Jazz Festival in 2016. The company took 2017 off to rethink how to bring more tourists to an Indian Wells music event.

The Tennis Garden festivals have been slowly moving away from the contemporary jazz that marked the many Desert Lexus events. Blues-rock singer Jonny Lang and Jon Batiste, music director for “The Late Show” with Stephen Colbert, headlined last year’s music festival.

This year’s lineup is another step up in name recognition. Four more support acts are expected to be announced in the next two weeks, but Lukas Nelson and Promise of the Real are riding a national crest as the backup band for Bradley Cooper in the critically acclaimed film, “A Star Is Born,” opening this weekend. Nelson is the son of country legend Willie Nelson and Promise of the Real regularly plays with Neil Young.

“I became a fan of Lukas Nelson pretty much the same way Bradley Cooper did,” Fitzgerald said, “seeing him at Desert Trip with Neil.”

Fitzgerald said Cooper approached Nelson back stage at the Desert Trip classic rock extravaganza at the Empire Polo Club in October 2016. Nelson not only gave him pointers on how to play guitar, he played for Cooper at the 2017 Stagecoach festival for footage for “A Star Is Born.” Cooper also filmed crowd shots when his co-star, Lady Gaga, headlined at Coachella that month. Nelson and Promise of the Real’s recent self-titled album features Gaga as a backup vocalist.

Nelson, who played with his father at the 2017 Stagecoach, returned as a featured performer with Promise of the Real at the 2018 Stagecoach. He'll headline Friday night at the Garden Jam while Guy will top the Saturday lineup

Guy, a Chicago blues guitarist who made his mark with the great Muddy Waters, also has generated attention with his most recent album, “The Blues Is Alive and Well.” It features Rolling Stones Mick Jagger and Keith Richards, and fellow British rocker Jeff Beck. Guy is known for playing guitar in the style of musicians he influenced, including Jimi Hendrix and Stevie Ray Vaughan.

Guy has performed in the Coachella Valley as part of a Hendrix tribute show with bassist Billy Cox at Fantasy Springs Resort Casino and as a headliner at the second Rhythm Wine & Brew Experience, both in Indio.

The remainder of the festival lineup is expected to feature multi-genre musical artists playing under a roof at the Tennis Garden pavilion.

Fitzgerald also said he will increase the festival amenities at the event, including art, merchandise booths and cuisine.

Tickets to the Garden Jam Music Festival are scheduled to go on sale at 10 a.m. Nov. 14. Information: GardenJamMusicFestival.com. Prices are expected to be announced in two weeks.

search_result.title: Coachella hasn't officially been canceled. What we know about April ...
search_result.text:
Amanda Ulrich

Palm Springs Desert Sun

Updated Dec. 8, 2020, 4:13 p.m. ET

With four months until the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival is set to take place, the chance the mega-concert will happen in spring 2021 is getting slimmer by the day.

While there's been no official confirmation of the festival's postponement, another major desert event in Palm Springs has already moved into Coachella's time slot for April. Coronavirus cases continue to spike across the state, prompting regional stay-at-home orders based on hospital capacity.

The Coachella website also features little to no detail about a 2021 concert. And the website of Goldenvoice, the company that produces the festival, now lists the 2021 Stagecoach festival, held the weekend after Coachella in Indio, California, as "TBD."

Coachella 2020 was initially pushed to October as COVID-19 swept California for the first time earlier this year. A few months later, it was postponed again to April 2021.

Several media reports this fall, triggered by a Rolling Stone article and comments from unnamed "music-industry insiders," speculated that Coachella would be delayed for a third time, until October 2021. That news has not been confirmed.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Goldenvoice has not made any updated announcement about the 2021 festival, and the city of Indio has not been told by Goldenvoice that the April date is off.

By this time of year, there are typically almost-weekly meetings between the city of Indio and Goldenvoice staff, representatives from regional utilities and Riverside County, security personnel and others, Indio City Manager Mark Scott told The Desert Sun.

"We're talking about planning meetings with 60 people in the room," he said.

"If (Goldenvoice) were to come in and say, 'This is gonna happen (in April),' because we've done it so many times it wouldn't be impossible on our end to respond to that," he added. "But I would wonder if it's already too late for them to do it from their end."

Locals plans for possibility of no April festival

Annual concerts and festivals prove to be a substantial windfall for Indio, which bears the tagline the "City of Festivals." It's home to roughly 20 major yearly events, from Coachella to the International Tamale Festival.

The two April music festivals alone bring about $106 million to the Indio economy, including spending at local businesses, according to a Coachella Valley Economic Partnership study from 2016. The city's government made more than $3.5 million in ticket fees and other taxes from Coachella and Stagecoach in 2019.

While Indio's approved fiscal 2020-21 budget from June includes potential revenue and expenditures for April 2021 festivals, the city's more current planning assumptions do not.

Evaluating the city's general fund without revenue from the April festivals is merely to be conservative, not an announcement that they will be postponed, Scott reiterated at an Indio City Council meeting last month.

"We're assuming for the sake of planning; we don't know this for a fact," he said. "I don't want any headlines saying I've announced there are no concerts in April. This is just simply a conservative planning assumption."

Modernism Week moves to April time slot

Modernism Week will move from its usual February spot on the calendar to April 8-18 in 2021, it was announced this month. The event typically draws tens of thousands of mid-century modern architecture fans to the Palm Springs area.

But those updated dates fall within Coachella's assumed 2021 weekends: April 9-11 and April 16-18. Modernism Week Executive Director Lisa Vossler Smith said organizers moved forward with the new schedule under the assumption that the festivals are "moving" to a later date.

Vossler Smith said the move would give Modernism Week "an opportunity to be able to accommodate visitors through hotels and home rentals. ... It’s an opportunity this year that we probably won’t have again."

She added: "We don’t plan on moving from February in the future. This is just an exception for this year which is obviously different."

search_result.title: Coachella, Stagecoach music festivals postponed by coronavirus

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.

search_result.title: Stagecoach 2018 is more than just Garthcoach - USA Today
search_result.text:
April 27, 2018, 7:32 a.m. ET

The move is already on to brand California’s Country Music Festival as “Garthcoach.”

After Beyoncé's dominance of Coachella on the same polo fields of Indio where Garth Brooks will perform Sunday night, it’s only natural to assume that the best-selling American recording artist of all time will have a similarly royal impact on Stagecoach.

Brooks, who has surpassed Elvis Presley with 148 million records sold, will close the three-day festival with an appearance by his wife, vocalist Trisha Yearwood. It's his first performance at Stagecoach and it follows a three-year concert tour that Billboard called the “top-selling North American tour ever logged by an artist.” This is his first show since that tour ended on Dec. 23 and he told Billboard then that he and his band had been running on empty for a year-and-a-half.

So, Stagecoach will get a re-charged performance from a guy who is astutely aware of every potential historical moment within his grasp.

But this Stagecoach also is making history for changes off the Mane Stage, where Brooks, Keith Urban and Florida Georgia Line are headlining daily bills of modern country acts. The two biggest secondary stages are moving away from some niche genres, such as bluegrass, oldies rock and rockabilly, to showcase emerging modern country stars.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

The Mustang tent, the site of such past eclectic treats as a Celtic jam by the Railers in 2014 and old-timey selections from the Carolina Chocolate Drops' Genuine Negro Jig album in 2012, is being replaced by the SiriusXM Spotlight Stage, which is being moved to a space in front of the Mane Stage.

Goldenvoice has long featured sponsored venues at its Coachella and Stagecoach festival, including the Heineken tent at Coachella and the Toyota attractions at Stagecoach. But SiriusXM is much more of a partner in the Spotlight Stage because it's not only helping Goldenvoice Director of Festival Talent Stacy Vee program the artists, it's amplifying their exposure with live broadcasts, including interviews and behind-the-scenes commentary, on SiriusXM's The Highway and Garth channels.

The Spotlight Stage will provide an opportunity for six up-and-coming artists per day to not only be heard on a satellite radio, but to be seen by many festival-goers who won't have to leave their seats and blankets. Spotlight Stage performers also have little competition from artists on other stages. Each act performs a 25-minute set from 12:05 to 3:50 p.m., and the first artists don’t start on the Mane Stage until 4 p.m.

The Palomino tent, featuring eclectic artists ranging from Southern rockers The Georgia Satellites to traditional country vocalist Ronnie Milsap, presents artists from 1:30 to 8:30 p.m. Friday and Saturday, and 1:30 to 7:40 p.m. on Sunday. But it’s only featuring two acts a day at the same time the Spotlight is presenting artists.

Vee has embraced a role in developing new artists at Stagecoach since Goldenvoice President and CEO Paul Tollett put her in charge of Stagecoach in 2015. She said SiriusXM shares her enthusiasm in using Stagecoach as a forum for new artists.

“SiriusXM is a partner in every sense of the word,” Vee said. “They have a long history of breaking artists first, have the ability to promote any kind of artist across their various country, country-related stations, and they are some of the nicest people I’ve had the pleasure of working with. They get it.

“It was a true collaboration in all respects. We talked about our favorite up-and-coming artists, artists they believed in, a new artist I had seen, and it all just kind of came together naturally. Thanks to SiriusXM, people are talking about Stagecoach from sea-to-shining-sea. You have no idea how exciting that is to us.”

The lineup on the Spotlight Stage includes several artists who have been fast-tracked by SiriusXM.

For example, Brown & Gray, a country-pop duo featuring Texas-born Kaci Brown and the British Sam Gray, was brought together by the management at Notting Hill Music, who got their song, Top Down, featured on Apple Music and Radio Disney Country. SiriusXM put the song on its country station, The Highway, and named them a Highway Find Artist. They then performed on The Highway Finds Fall 2017 tour and this year, CMT (the country equivalent of MTV) selected Brown & Gray for its Discovery Artist program. Now they’ll perform at 12:45 p.m. Sunday.

For another example, Adam Doleac, started playing music and writing songs while playing baseball at the University of Southern Mississippi. His posted songs attracted the attention of some Nashville songwriters and he moved to Music City to further his career. In 2014, SiriusXM selected his song, I Put It On Ya, as an On The Horizon track on The Highway and he was offered a publishing deal with Sony/ATV, followed by a management deal and a self-titled debut EP last June. Doleac’s website says, “Sirius XM continues to be one of Adam’s most significant supporters. They have played multiple tracks from his EP on The Highway, and Adam was a featured artist on the fall 2017 Highway Finds Tour." He’ll play at 12:45 p.m. Sunday on the Spotlight Stage.

Doleac also is one of many artists in the Spotlight and Palomino tents recognized by Rolling Stone’s monthyly 10 New Country Artists You Need to Know. Others include:

Jade Bird, a 20-year-old British singer-songwriter recognized by Rolling Stone last July. She’s performing at 12:45 p.m. Friday.

Other notable Palomino acts include:
