search_result.title: Lady Antebellum: 'We are definitely really country' - USA Today
search_result.text:
Bruce Fessier, USA TODAY

Updated April 28, 2013, 10:23 p.m. ET

Coachella or Stagecoach%3F Charles Kelley wasn%27t sure

The group%27s new album%2C %27Golden%2C%27 arrives May 7

Trying to get back on pop radio was %22the most stressed out I%27ve ever been%2C%22 Charles Kelley says

INDIO, Calif. — Charles Kelley is exhorting a crowd of fans 40,000 strong with his band, Lady Antebellum.

"Sounding good, Coachella!" the lanky singer/songwriter shouts.

The only problem is, he's at Stagecoach: California's Country Music Festival. The Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival was held the previous two weekends at this polo field.

Kelley and his bandmates, vocalist Hillary Scott and singer/guitarist Dave Haywood, could be forgiven for mixing up their destinations. They traveled by bus for roughly nine hours from northern California to the Southern California desert. They barely had time to set up a table to satiate their passion for Ping-Pong before doing a series of interviews and fan meet-and-greets before a 9:30 p.m. show.

But Lady Antebellum probably could have played Coachella. Not only has the mammoth indie music festival recently featured bands that blur the line between pop and country such as The Lumineers, Mumford & Sons and the Avett Brothers, but Lady Antebellum crossed that line with a paradigm leap when the band won a 2011 Grammy for record of the year for the single Need You Now.

STORY:Stagecoach blazes with high temps, Jones tributes

But as Lady Antebellum prepares for the May 7 release of Golden, its second album of original music since Need You Now, the band members seem to be eschewing pop to focus on country music.

When they tried to consciously cross over again on third album Own the Night, it didn't connect with pop audiences. It won a Grammy for best country album, but it didn't come easy.

"We had a door open that we weren't really planning on opening with pop radio (on Need You Now)," Kelley says. "So, let's be honest. We wanted to see if there was a chance we could get back on it. We went into the third record thinking that could be a possibility."

Adds Haywood: "We started to play radio and marketing, and 'This will work here' and 'This will work there,' and that's never been great for us."

Says Kelley: "There was so much tug and pull and so much opportunity that you wanted to go out and chase it, and it was the most stressed out I've ever been in my life. I don't think we were really able to appreciate it until afterward."

For Golden, the band decided to stop thinking like marketing executives and get back to writing songs as they used to: three people sitting around in a room, often taking ideas from band members or submissions from outsiders that seemed to fit their lives.

Consequently, Golden feels like the rightful follow-up to the Need You Now CD. It's deeply personal, with songs about leaving a town representing their past (Goodbye Town), supporting a friend with a broken heart (I Can't Stand the Rain) and explaining the male and female sides of a breakup (All for Love).

The first single, Downtown, is the band's most significant departure. It's about a girl who wants her complacent lover to take her to downtown to get crazy — which is at odds with Scott's real life, since she's heading into her seventh month of pregnancy and making the Nashville home she shares with her husband, Lady A drummer Chris Tyrrell, more fit for a little girl.

The album's opening track, Get to Me, sounds like the logical successor to Need You Now.

"It's funny you said that," Kelley says. "I've actually said that about that song: 'It has that same urgency. Get to me! I need you now! Get to me now!' I think people love hearing that urgency of love. We're hoping that will end up being a single, too."

Get to Me was submitted by Hillary Lindsey and James T. Slater more than three years ago but somehow got lost in the shuffle. Scott says with a laugh that she definitely wasn't singing about the same person she was singing to in Need You Now, but when she heard it while considering songs for Golden, it jumped out at her.

"It's definitely a place where I've been in my life where you're just really missing the person you love," Scott says. "Some of my biggest influences as a female vocalist are Bonnie Raitt and Trisha Yearwood, and I truly listened to this song and thought, 'How would Trisha Yearwood sing this song?' And I really put myself in the song. I wanted to get that emotion out because it is such a relatable subject matter."

Golden also reflects the social significance the trio feels in real life. The band has created a LadyAID Fund of the Community Foundation of Middle Tennessee to support children in need through charities such as St. Jude Children's Research Hospital and UNHCR: the UN Refugee Agency. They're planning a "Ping-Pong and Songs" benefit for LadyAID at CMA Music Festival in Nashville in June.

Generation Away gives shout-outs to Martin Luther King and Johnny Cash and reflects the group's desire for Millennials to influence their society as the '60s generation did.

"You don't have to be famous to make a mark; just let's try to have a moment," Kelley says. "At Woodstock, everybody was there and everybody was loving each other. Let's have this be a moment: Tonight. Let's make a mark on society."

But the album is titled Golden because the bandmates feel as if they're in a space where they can finally enjoy the success from Need You Now. All three are married and looking at their music as a career and an opportunity to have an impact. That's one reason they decided to stop chasing crossover success and just perform for an audience that's more likely to be loyal for the long run than the fickle pop audience.

"We feel loyal to the country market," Kelley says. "I don't know how well we would fit in between Bruno Mars and Phoenix. It's so funny. We think maybe we're not the country-est thing in the world until we've played some shows with pop artists. Then it's like, 'Yep. We are definitely really country.' So we feel comfortable in this genre and we want to be loyal to the genre."

Bruce Fessier writes daily for The (Palm Springs) Desert Sun.

search_result.title: Has Justin Rose won the Masters? Golfer's history at Augusta National
search_result.text:
Updated April 12, 2026, 6:58 p.m. ET

Editor's Note: This story has been updated with new information. Follow along with live updates of all the final-round action at the Masters Tournament.

In 2025, Rory McIlroy finally secured his long-awaited, coveted green jacket at Augusta National Golf Club after coming so close to it several times before.

Justin Rose nearly followed in McIlroy's footsteps in the 90th edition of the Masters this year.

The Englishman once again put himself in a position to challenge for the title in the final round Sunday, April 12, but a few miscues on the back nine kept him from securing his green jacket. There was a brief period where he was in first place after a flurry of birdies to end the front nine, but McIlroy surpassed him again and two-time Masters champion Scottie Scheffler also leapfrogged him on the back nine.

Rose finished in a four-way tie for third with Tyrrell Hatton, Russell Henley and Cameron Young at 10-under par. McIlroy won his second consecutive Masters with a score of -12. Sheffler finished runner up, one stroke back.

2026 MASTERS RESULTS: Final leaderboard from Augusta National

Rose nearly reached golf immortality last season when he rallied with a the back-nine tear, shooting 6-under 66 to get into a playoff with McIlroy, who eventually won on the first extra hole.

"I'm comfortable here but never complacent," Rose said after his third round on Saturday where he shot a 3-under 69. "I feel like there's always something to learn, something to push for. You've always got to respect the golf course.

Here's what to know about Rose's history at Augusta National in the Masters:

Justin Rose scorecard at Masters today

Here's a look at Rose's scorecard from Sunday's final round at The Masters:

Birdie 3 Par 5 Bogey 5 Par 3 Birdie 3 Par 3 Birdie 3 Birdie 4 Birdie 3 Par 4 Bogey 5 Bogey 4 Par 5 Par 4 Birdie 4 Par 3 Bogey 5 Par 4

Has Justin Rose won the Masters?

No, Rose has not received a green jacket in his career. He has, however, come close to winning at Augusta National on three separate occasions, including in last year's playoff loss to McIlroy.

He is one of nine golfers to finish as the runner-up at least three times in the history of the Masters. He first finished as the runner-up in 2015, when he finished in a tie for second with Phil Mickelson and four strokes behind Jordan Spieth. He then finished as the runner-up in 2017 when he lost the first hole of the playoff with Sergio Garcia.

Justin Rose finishes at the Masters

The 2026 Masters Tournament is Rose's 21st time playing at Augusta National. He's now finished in the top 25 in 16 of 21 trips to Augusta National.

Here's a look at Rose's last finishes at the Masters going back to 2020:

Score in parentheses

2020: Tied for 23rd (-5)

Tied for 23rd (-5) 2021: Tied for 7th (-5)

Tied for 7th (-5) 2022: Missed cut (+8)

Missed cut (+8) 2023: Tied for 16th (-2)

Tied for 16th (-2) 2024: Missed cut (+7)

Missed cut (+7) 2025: Runner-up (-11)

Runner-up (-11) 2026: Tied for 3rd (-10)

search_result.title: 3 golfers have won back-to-back Masters. Can Rory McIlroy join them?
search_result.text:
April 12, 2026, 2:39 p.m. ET

2025 Masters winner Rory McIlroy entered the final round of this year's tournament tied for the lead, despite shooting 1-over on Saturday, April 11. If he manages to pull ahead and claim victory once again, he will join a very exclusive list of golfers to have won at Augusta National two years in a row.

The Masters has been played every year since 1934 (with the exception of 1943 to 1945 due to World War II). Since then, only three golfers have ever won consecutive tournaments, and they're among the best of the best to ever play the sport.

Jack Nicklaus (1965-1966 champion), Nick Faldo (1989-1990), and Tiger Woods (2001-2002) have all won at least three Masters titles, with Nicklaus and Woods winning six and five, respectively. Each of them have carved their names into Augusta's history books forever, and McIlroy has the opportunity to do the same today.

Jack Nicklaus' back-to-back Masters titles

Although Nicklaus won the titles in 1965 and 1966, those weren't his first Masters wins.

His first came in 1963, at just 23 years old. Nicklaus finished the tournament at 2-under, which was good enough for the win. His best round was the second, where he shot an incredible 66, three strokes better than the next best round by anyone else in the field.

His back-to-back wins were far more impressive though, with Nicklaus shooting under 70 in three of the four rounds in 1965, winning the tournament by nine strokes over the second-place finishers: Gary Player and Arnold Palmer.

In 1966, Nicklaus struggled a bit more. After a stellar 4-under in the first round to lead after Day 1, he fell back a bit, and was forced into a playoff with Gary Brewer and Tommy Jacobs for the win. The trio played an entire round on Monday, with Nicklaus shooting 2-under to win, while Jacobs and Brewer shot even par and 6-over respectively.

Nick Faldo's back-to-back Masters wins

Unlike Nicklaus, Faldo's win at the 1989 Masters was the first of his career. Although he had previously won another major, taking home the 1987 Open Championship title.

The Englishman was forced into a playoff with Scott Hoch to secure the 1989 green jacket. Faldo won the playoff on the 11th hole, a hole he'd bogeyed all four rounds of the tournament. He birdied the hole in the playoff.

Faldo's victory in 1990 was equally suspenseful, with another playoff needed for the win. This time, his opponent was Raymond Floyd, another previous Masters winner himself. Once again, Faldo won the tournament on Hole 11, recording a par this time around.

Tiger Woods' back-to-back Masters titles

The 2001 Masters Tournament is arguably the pinnacle of Tiger Woods' illustrious career. With his victory that year, Woods completed the "Tiger Slam", where Woods held all four major titles. He won the tournament at 16-under, two strokes ahead of second-place David Duval.

This wasn't the first Masters win of Woods' career, but the fact that he completed a Grand Slam in a single calendar year remains one of the greatest feats in the history of golf.

Woods' 2002 victory was slightly less exciting, with Vijay Singh shooting just even par in the third round after shooting a 65 the day prior. Singh held a four-stroke lead over Woods heading into Saturday's round, but ended the day in third-place, two strokes back of Tiger Woods and Retief Goosen. Goosen shot a 2-over 74 during the final round, all but handing Woods another green jacket.

Woods' 12-under to win set the record for the best score by a defending champion at the Masters.

search_result.title: Looking back at 2002 Masters: Winner, purse, highlights and more
search_result.text:
March 18, 2025, 6:03 a.m. ET

Tiger Woods won the 2002 Masters Tournament, marking his second consecutive Masters victory.

Significant changes were made to the Augusta National Golf Course in 2002, including lengthening nine holes and adjusting fairway and bunker layouts.

Woods earned $1,008,000 from the total tournament purse of $5,471,320.

The 2025 Masters begins in 23 days.

As the countdown to heading down Magnolia Lane continues, Golfweek is taking a look back at some Masters history through the years.

Today, it's time to examine the 2002 Masters and the significant milestones that occurred at Augusta National Golf Club that year.

Who won the 2002 Masters?

Tiger Woods becomes the third player to capture consecutive Masters titles. Woods joined Jack Nicklaus and Nick Faldo as the only players, to this day, to win consecutive Masters titles. Woods shoots under par each round and takes home the first seven-figure, first-place prize in Masters history.

It's also the year nine holes were changed on the golf course, the most all-time.

2002 Masters leaderboard

1) Tiger Woods; 12-under 276

2) Retief Goosen; 9-under 279

3) Phil Mickelson; 8-under 280

4) Jose Maria Olazabal; 7-under 281

T5) Ernie Els, Padraig Harrington; 6-under 282

2002 Masters purse, prize money

Woods won $1,008,000, and the total purse was $5,471,320.

Who was low amateur at the 2002 Masters?

There was no low amateur in 2002, as none made the cut.

Augusta National course changes in 2002

Redesigned and lengthened Masters tees on nine holes; several other fairway and bunker changes.Course yardage increased to 7,270.

No. 1: Masters tees moved back 20-25 yards. Fairway bunker reshaped and extended 10-15 yards towards the green. Portion of fairway landing area regraded.

No. 7: Masters tees moved back 40-45 yards. Portion of fairway landing area regraded.

No. 8: Masters tees moved back 15-20 yards and shifted to golfer’s right 10 yards. Reshaped and nearlydoubled the size of the fairway bunker.

No. 9: Masters tees moved back 25-30 yards.

No. 10: Masters tees moved back 5-10 yards and moved to the golfer’s left five yards.

No. 11: Masters tees moved back 30-35 yards and moved five yards to golfer’s right. Portion of fairway landing area recontoured.

No. 13: Masters tees moved back 20-25 yards.

No. 14: Masters tees moved back 30-35 yards.

No. 18: Masters tees moved back 55-60 yards and moved to the golfer’s right five yards. Bunker complexadjusted, making bunkers approximately 10 percent larger. Trees added left of fairway bunkers.

Who won the 2002 Masters Par 3 Contest?

Nick Price won his only Par 3 Contest, shooting 5-under 22.

2002 Masters facts, stats

search_result.title: 70 Masters golf tournaments later, and no ... - The Augusta Chronicle
search_result.text:
David Westin

Special to the Augusta Chronicle

Updated April 9, 2022, 11:21 a.m. ET

There might be patrons who have a longer active streak of attending the Masters Tournament than Augusta’s Dan LaSure Sr., but’s he’s never heard of them.

Throwing out the COVID-19 Masters in 2020, when no outside fans were allowed, this is LaSure’s 70th consecutive Masters. He was even here in 2021, when attendance was limited because of the pandemic.

“I went over 50 years without missing a day (of tournament play),” LaSure said on Friday at Augusta National Golf Club. “Sometimes I’d come seven straight days. I just love the place. It’s like being in heaven.”

LaSure, who will be 89 in June, attended his first Masters in 1952, which was won by Sam Snead. Back then, a badge was $10 and included the practice rounds, tournament rounds, parking and a program. A four-day tournament series badge is $375 this year.

To put it in historical context, when LaSure started attending the Masters, it was not televised, none of the three bridges (the Hogan on No. 12, the Nelson on No. 13 and the Sarazen on No. 15) had been dedicated and the Par 3 Course did not exist.

In 1952, the same year of LaSure’s first Masters, the Champions Dinner started. The following year he watched Ben Hogan win his second Masters.

“It means quite a bit to me,” LaSuresaid. “It’s like the beginning of spring. If you play golf, you can’t wait to get here.”

On Friday, LaSure was at the course with his oldest son, Danny, who is 60 and lives in Asheville, N.C. The spry LaSure still walks the course.

“He’s hard to keep up with,” Danny said. “We love No. 13, 16 and especially Amen Corner. It’s fun to get to share this with him. I enjoy just being with him. I learn something from him every year.”

LaSure Sr. is already thinking about the 2024 Masters, when he will be 90.

“I’ll keep coming back as long as I can amble around here,” he said. “I will admit the hills have gotten higher.”

LaSure Sr. was 18 years old, attending Richmond Academy and working at Cullums on Broad Street in Augusta when the clothing store opened a small booth at Augusta National for the Masters in 1952. He helped out in the booth, liked what he saw and has come back every year since.

For 25 years, he worked the scoreboard adjacent to the 13th green, overlooking the 14th tee.

“It’s such a glorious hole,” LaSure said.

He saw some of the greatest shots in tournament history, but it’s a dubious one that he remembers the best. He was the one who posted an update to the score of Tsuneyuki “Tommy” Nakajima when he made 13 on the hole in 1978. It still stands as the highest score on that hole.

He later worked the scoreboard to the left of the par-3 sixth hole.

The two greatest shots during LaSure’s time at the Masters, he says, were Tiger Woods’ chip-in on the 16th hole on the way to victory in 2005 and Bubba’s Watson escape from the right woods on No. 10 to win a sudden-death playoff in 2012.

Like so many, LaSure is a Woods fan and has gained even more respect for the way he’s battled back from a car accident 14 months ago to play this week.

“His playing in the tournament this week is amazing to me. He’s the man. Everybody loves him,” LaSure said.

He picks the 1987 Masters as his favorite because it was the only one won by an Augusta native.

“I was real glad when Larry Mize won,” he said.

He regrets not saving all his scorer’s and series badges.

“I didn’t start saving them till real late," he said, "because I didn’t think much about the fact that they would be something I should have saved."

search_result.title: Masters back-to-back champions: Here's who did it at Augusta
search_result.text:
April 11, 2026, 2:33 p.m. ET

The first ever Masters tournament at Augusta National Golf Club was held in 1934. Since its inception, there has only ever been three individuals to win the Masters tournament in consecutive years.

Defending champion Rory McIlroy put on a performance through the first two rounds that has him leading by six strokes. He looks in prime shape to position himself to be the fourth-ever back-to-back champion.

The tournament's only back-to-back champions consist of Jack Nicklaus, Nick Faldo and Tiger Woods.

Nicklaus, who also holds the record for most Masters victories with six, was the first ever back-to-back champion claiming the honor in 1965 and 1966.

Faldo won three Masters overall, but became the second to win it in consecutive years in 1989 and 1990.

Woods, who became the youngest Masters winner in 1997, won a total of five Masters tournaments. He joined the lucrative list of back-to-back Masters champions when he won the Augusta National course in 2001 and 2002.

McIlroy can become the first person in two decades to become a repeat Masters champion, the first since Woods. McIlroy will begin Round 3 in a pairing with Sam Burns, teeing off at 2:50 p.m. ET in Group 27.

Back-to-back Masters champions

Jack Nicklaus: 1965 and 1966.

Nick Faldo: 1989 and 1990.

Tiger Woods: 2001 and 2002.

search_result.title: Masters 2026 leaderboard: Rory McIlroy holds on after late drama
search_result.text:
Updated April 12, 2026, 9:21 p.m. ET

It wasn't always pretty, but Rory McIlroy ran it back at Augusta National.

McIlroy carded a 2-under 70 on Sunday to give him the win at 13-under, making him just the fourth golfer in history to win the event in back-to-back years.

McIlroy, who headed into Saturday's Round 3 of the Masters with a seemingly insurmountable six-stroke lead and gave it all back in one day, fought through an uneven final round to claim his second consecutive green jacket.

USA TODAY Sports had frequent live updates during Sunday's final round from Augusta with scores, leaderboard updates, highlights and analysis:

How many majors has Rory McIlroy won?

McIlroy's victory is the sixth major championship of his career. That ties him with Lee Trevino, Phil Mickelson, and Nick Faldo (another back-to-back Masters winner) for the 12th-most all-time.

Two-time Masters winner Scottie Scheffler finished close behind at 11-under. Scheffler had a phenomenal final two days. In fact, his third round score of 65 was the best he'd ever carded in his 27 rounds playing the Masters. He tallied zero bogeys on Saturday or Sunday. Still, it was not enough to catch McIlroy. When all was said and done, two of the game's greatest golfers stood side-by-side on the leaderboard when all was said and done.

Masters payout and prize money

Although the green jacket is the ultimate prize, the money that comes with it is pretty sweet as well. Here is what each of the top-ten finishers earned based on how they finished.

1. Rory McIlroy (-13) — $4,500,000

2. Scottie Scheffler (-11) — $2,430,000

T3. Tyrrell Hatton (-10) — $1,080,000

T3. Russell Henley (-10) — $1,080,000

T3. Justin Rose (-10) — $1,080,000

T3. Cameron Young (-10) — $1,080,000

T7. Collin Morikawa (-9) — $725,625

T7. Sam Burns (-9) — $725,625

T9. Max Homa (-8) — $630,000

T9. Xander Schauffele (-8) — $630,000

Masters leaderboard live (updated 6:55 p.m.)

Rory McIlroy is two holes away from history at Augusta National. Several challengers arose over the last 24 hours, from Cameron Young to Justin Rose to a late push from world No. 1 Scottie Scheffler, but McIlroy still holds a two-shot lead into the 17th hole.

1. Rory McIlroy -13 (F)

2. Scottie Scheffler -11 (F)

T3. Justin Rose -10 F7)

T3. Cameron Young -10 (16)

T3. Russell Henley -10 (F)

T3. Tyrell Hatton -10 (F)

Scottie Scheffler finishes with a score of 68

Scheffler's par on the 18th and final hole puts him in a tie for second place with 11-under par at the end of his round. The world No. 1 put up a valiant effort in the third and fourth rounds to come all the way back from 24th place and is the first bogey-free player in the final two days since 1942.

But barring any last-minute surprises, Rory McIlroy is in position to make history with his second consecutive green jacket.

Masters leaderboard live (updated 6:00 p.m.)

Rory McIlroy has raced out to a two-shot lead, but world No. 1 Scottie Scheffler has climbed all the way up the leaderboard into a tie for second place with two holes left to go. Justin Rose has cooled off on the back nine, but is still in position to contend at 11-under.

1. Rory McIlroy -13 (14)

T2. Scottie Scheffler -11 (16)

T2. Justin Rose -11 (15)

T4. Cameron Young -10 (14)

T4. Russell Henley -10 (17)

T4. Tyrell Hatton -10 (F)

Rory McIlroy extends lead with second straight birdie

McIlroy's starting to catch fire with another birdie, his second consecutive and fifth of the day, on the 13th hole. He adds another shot to his lead and builds some more breathing room heading into the home stretch.

Scottie Scheffler moves into a tie for 2nd place on Masters leaderboard

It looked like Scheffler was headed towards disaster on the 15th hole when his drive landed in the treeline and his next shot clipped one of the branches. But somehow, Scheffler recovered for one of the most remarkable birdies you'll ever see on the par-5 hole.

That shot leaps Scheffler up in the leaderboards from sixth into a tie for second place, three strokes within Rory McIlroy.

Rory McIlroy hits birdie on 12th hole

McIlroy hit as close to a perfect tee shot as you could get on the par-3 12th hole. The ball sailed over the water and landed just under seven feet away from the cup to set up his fourth birdie of the final round. Now at 12-under par, McIlroy is now two shots clear of Justin Rose.

Masters leaderboard live (updated 5:00 p.m.)

Rory McIlroy has a share of the lead again after a bogey from Rose on the 11th hole. Cameron Young had an opportunity to join them atop the leaderboard, but smoked a long putt for birdie on No. 10 and settled for par.

Rose birdied again on the No. 12 to give McIlroy sole possession of the top spot on the leaderboard.

1. Rory McIlroy -11 (10)

T2. Justin Rose -10 (12)

T2. Cameron Young -10 (10)

T2. Russell Henley -10 (14)

T2. Tyrell Hatton -10 (F)

6. Scottie Scheffler -9 (12)

Cameron Young opens back nine with missed opportunity to re-take share of lead

Sunday has been a mixed bag for Young, who briefly reclaimed a share of the lead after showing off some touch to set up a birdie on the eighth hole. But his second bogey of the day on the very next hole bumped him back down to third to finish the first nine at 10-under par.

Young had a chance to start the back nine on the right foot with a birdie, but missed the long putt and settled for par to keep him at +1 on the day.

Masters leaderboard live (updated 4:20 p.m.)

We have a new leader at Augusta. Justin Rose has birdied three consecutive holes to move into the lead by himself as he makes the turn to the back nine.

At age 45, Rose is looking to become the second-oldest player to win the Masters, behind only Jack Nicklaus, who was 46 when he won the tournament 40 years ago.

Rose rolled in birdies on 7, 8 and 9 to get to 12 under for the tournament, two shots ahead of Russell Henley, Cameron Young and Rory McIlroy.

1. Justin Rose -12 (9)

T2. Cameron Young -10 (7)

T2. Russell Henley -10 (10)

T2. Rory McIlroy -10 (7)

T5. Scottie Scheffler -9 (10)

T5. Tyrell Hatton -9 (15)

Check out the full leaderboard and tee times

Justin Rose (11-under through 8) takes over the lead

Thanks to consecutive birdies from leader Cameron Young, the outright lead belongs to last year's Masters runner-up Justin Rose.

Rose added another birdie on the par-5 eighth hole to his incredible birdie on 7 to move to 11 under par − one stroke ahead of Young, Russell Henley and Rory McIlroy.

Masters leaderboard update (3:46 p.m.)

With a birdie on the second hole, Cameron Young took over the outright lead at 12 under par. Rory McIlroy rolled in a birdie putt on No. 3 to pull back into a tie, but gave two shots back on the following hole with a 3-putt from nine feet.

Justin Rose has moved into a tie for second at minus 10 after hitting an incredible approach on No. 7 out of the pine straw to within a foot for birdie.

Russell Henley birdied both No. 7 and No. 8 to get to 10 under as well. Meanwhile. Scottie Scheffler is still lurking after birdieing three of his first five holes.

1. Cameron Young -12 (5)

T2. Rory McIlroy -10 (5)

T2. Justin Rose -10 (7)

T2. Russell Henley -10 (8)

5. Scottie Scheffler -9 (7)

6. Sam Burns -8 (6)

Check out the full leaderboard and tee times

Rory McIlroy takes an early hit with improbable double bogey on 4

Missing the green on the par-3 fourth hole, Rory McIlroy had to pitch over a bunker to get his ball on the putting surface.

He did that, cozying his ball up to within nine feet. However, he pushed his par putt past the left edge of the hole to drop him out of a tie for the lead with Cameron Young. But that wasn't the end of it.

McIlroy's two-footer for bogey caught the left edge of the cup but was a tad too strong and it spun out, forcing him to tap in for a double-bogey 5.

Rory McIlroy live scorecard

Rory McIlroy and Cameron Young, in the final pairing of the day at 2:25 p.m. ET, have hit their tee shots on the opening hole and are on the course.

RORY MCILROY LIVE TRACKER: Follow Rory McIlroy hole-by-hole on Sunday

Scottie Scheffler live scorecard

Scottie Scheffler and Haotong Li are off the first tee in Sunday's final round at the Masters. They had the 1:52 p.m. ET tee time on hole No. 1, the 445-yard par 4.

Both golfers begin the day at 7 under par, four shots behind the leaders.

Scheffler got off to a great start, sticking his approach shot on No. 1 to eight feet. He converted the birdie putt to move one shot closer. He then followed it up with another birdie on No. 3.

The charge may be just beginning for the world's No. 1 player.

SCOTTIE SCHEFFLER LIVE TRACKER: Follow Scottie Scheffler hole-by-hole on Sunday

Masters leaderboard update (3:15 p.m.)

With a birdie on the second hole, Cameron Young took over the outright lead at 12 under par. But Rory McIlroy rolled in a birdie putt on No. 3 to pull back into a tie for the lead.

Meanwhile. Scottie Scheffler has birdied three of his first five holes to get to within two shots of the lead.

Sam Burns did briefly move into a tie for the lead with a birdie on his first hole. However, Burns took a double-bogey 7 on the second hole and another bogey on the third.

T1. Cameron Young -12 (3)

T1. Rory McIlroy -12 (3)

3. Scottie Scheffler -10 (5)

T4. Justin Rose -9 (5)

Check out the full leaderboard and tee times

Keegan Bradley, Gary Woodland shoot 66

The best rounds of the day among the golfers teeing off early came from Americans Keegan Bradley and Gary Woodland, who shot 6-under 66s.

Woodland briefly had a 7-under round going before bogeying No. 17.

Bradley began the day 3 over, but carded four birdies on the front side and four more on the back (to counter two bogeys) for his 66.

Masters leaderboard update (2:44 p.m.)

Rory McIlroy and Cameron Young are the final golfers off the first tee as the final round of the Masters is now in full swing. And we have a three-way tie at the top.

Sam Burns moved quickly to pull even with McIlroy and Young with a birdie on No. 1. Meanwhile, Scottie Scheffler got his round off to a great start with a birdie on the first and another on the third to move to 9 under for the tournament, just two shots back.

T1. Cameron Young -11 (1)

T1. Rory McIlroy -11 (1)

T4. Justin Rose -9 (1)

T4. Scottie Scheffler -9 (3)

T6. Jason Day -8 (2)

T6. Shane Lowry -8 (1)

T6. Haotong Li -8 (3)

Check out the full leaderboard and tee times

Masters leaderboard update (2:15 p.m.)

Rory McIlroy and Cameron Young enter Sunday's final round of the Masters tied atop the leaderboard at 11 under par. McIlroy is trying to defend his 2025 title, while Young is looking to win his first major championship.

Scottie Scheffler got his round off to a great start with a birdie on No. 1 to move to 8 under for the tournament, just three shots back. Justin Rose also chipped in for birdie on his opening hole.

T1. Cameron Young -11 (2:25 p.m.)

T1. Rory McIlroy -11 (2:25 p.m.)

3. Sam Burns -10 (2:14 p.m.)

T4. Shane Lowry -9 (2:14 p.m.)

T4. Justin Rose -9 (1)

T6. Scottie Scheffler -8 (1)

T6. Jason Day -8 (2:03 p.m.)

Check out the full leaderboard and tee times

Hole to watch: Keep an eye on No. 7

We'll get an indication of who might be prepared to make a move on Sunday when they get to the 450-yard, par-4 seventh hole. The Sunday pin placement is in the front right part of the green, at the bottom of a gradual slope.

If the players can hit toward the back center of the green, they can spin it back down the hill and get very close to the hole for birdie ... or better.

Aaron Rai did exactly that when his approach from 137 yards early in the day rolled back down and into the hole for an eagle 2.

Then a little over three hours later, Tyrell Hatton followed suit by holing out from 131 yards to take him to 6 under for the tournament.

Interestingly enough, No. 7 was the hardest hole on the course during Thursday's first round.

Masters leaderboard update (as of 1:47 p.m.)

Rory McIlroy and Cameron Young enter Sunday's final round of the Masters tied atop the leaderboard at 11 under par. McIlroy is trying to defend his 2025 title, while Young is looking to win his first major championship.

Viktor Hovland is playing the best among the golfers who teed off early, making the turn in 5-under 31 to get to 4 under par. He has since added birdies on No. 11 and No. 13 to get to within five shots of the lead.

All tee times Eastern

T1. Cameron Young -11 (2:25 p.m.)

T1. Rory McIlroy -11 (2:25 p.m.)

3. Sam Burns -10 (2:14 p.m.)

4. Shane Lowry -9 (2:14 p.m.)

T5. Jason Day -8 (2:03 p.m.)

T5. Justin Rose -8 (2:03 p.m.)

T7. Haotong Li -7 (1:52 p.m.)

T7. Scottie Scheffler -7 (1:52 p.m.)

T9. Viktor Hovland -6 (14)

T9. 5 others at -6

Viktor Hovland showing low scores are possible

The leading contender for round of the day on Sunday comes courtesy of Viktor Hovland.

The low amateur at the 2017 Masters, Hovland began his final round 1 over par, but used a birdie on hole No. 2 as a springboard to what could be a precursor of things to come. Under pristine conditions, Hovland added birdies on Nos. 3, 7, 8, 9, 11 and 13 to get to 7 under par for the day and 6 under for the tournament. That put him just five shots off the lead with five holes to play.

If Hovland and Gary Woodland − who posted a 6-under 66 earlier in the day − can go low, who knows what kind of a shootout we might have when the leaders tee off.

Brennan: Rory McIlroy right where he needs to be

Defending Masters champion Rory McIlroy showed up for the third round at Augusta National Golf Club with the largest 36-hole lead in tournament history.

In less than three hours on the course, it was gone.

But Rory McIlroy is Rory McIlroy and the Masters is the Masters, so the story of his day was far from over. He finished tied for the lead with the surging Cameron Young, having squandered that historic six-stroke lead heading into the final round.

He barely sounded chagrined. He wasn’t satisfied by any means, but he wasn’t particularly unhappy. He is right where he wants to be, writes columnist Christine Brennan.

Jon Rahm sizzles on front nine, shoots 33

Unlike his final round playing partner, Sergio Garcia, fellow Spaniard Jon Rahm is having an excellent fourth round. After bogeying the par-3 sixth, Rahm reeled off three consecutive birdies to close out his outward nine.

Rahm, the 2023 Masters champ, began the day at 5 over, but recorded five total birdies on the front (vs. two bogeys) to make the turn at 2 over for the tournament.

Sergio Garcia frustrated early, breaks driver

Sergio Garcia, the 2017 Masters champion, was one of the first golfers to tee off Sunday − beginning his round at 5 over par for the tournament. Things quickly went south as he bogeyed the first hole, and then hit a poor drive on the par-5 second hole.

However, his reaction was a bit over-the-top.

He also smashed his club against a cooler right afterward, breaking the head of his driver.

Garcia did manage to salvage par on the hole, but followed that with two more bogeys on 3 and 4.

Masters weather forecast for Sunday's final round

It's going to be another picture-perfect day on the grounds of Augusta National Golf Club for the final round of the Masters. Now, will the action on the course heat up the way the afternoon temperatures will?

Scoring conditions should be excellent as sunny skies and just a slight breeze prevail throughout the day. Temperatures should rise from 63 in the morning to the upper 80s by the time the leaders hit Amen Corner.

First golfers hit the course in Round 4

Aaron Rai and Charl Schwartzel were the first pairing off the tee as Sunday's final round of the Masters is officially underway. They began the day 18 and 20 shots behind the leaders, so the drama isn't likely to kick in for a little while.

Where to watch the Masters on Sunday

The final round of the Masters will air on CBS with coverage starting at 2 p.m. ET. Coverage will end at approximately 7 p.m. ET.

Anyone looking to stream Masters content can find coverage on Paramount+, Masters.com and the Masters App. You can also stream the final round on Fubo.

Back to back Masters winners

Only three golfers have ever won back-to-back Masters, Tiger Woods being the last in 2001-02. No golfer has won three straight.

Food at the Masters

At the Augusta National concession stands, three items have slightly increased in price this year. The blueberry muffin and southern cheese straws are both up to $2.50 from $2 a year ago, and cookies are now up to $2, a 25-cent increase from last year.

However, all the other traditional Augusta National staples will stay at the same prices as they were in 2025. In fact, the price of the egg salad and pimento cheese sandwiches hasn't increased since 2002.

Masters location: Where is Augusta National?

The 90th edition of the Masters Tournament will get underway this week, and for the 90th iteration in a row, it will take place at the same golf course in Georgia.

Augusta National Golf Club has hosted every Masters Tournament dating back to the first one in 1934. That makes Augusta National (and the Masters) unique among golf majors. No other major tournament – those being the U.S. Open, PGA Championship and British Open – has taken place exclusively at the same golf club. — Jack McKessy

search_result.title: Rory McIlroy earns Masters green jacket after Masters 2026 win
search_result.text:
Updated April 12, 2026, 7:53 p.m. ET

Rory McIlroy became the latest golfer to win back-to-back Masters tournaments and was presented with the prestigious green jacket for the second consecutive year.

He joins an exclusive club, becoming one of four golfers to win back-to-back Masters tournaments along with Jack Nicklaus (1965-66), Nick Faldo (1989-90) and Tiger Woods (2001-02).

"My parting message last year was, I can't wait to come back next year and put the green jacket on myself," McIlroy said jokingly. "I wasn't quite correct, because the chairman did it, but I did want to come back here and prove last year wasn't a fluke. It is an absolutely incredible golf tournament."

McIlroy delivered a promising start to the event but had to overcome a series of hiccups through the final two days.

He managed to overcome a slow start on Saturday, briefly giving up the lead before rallying back from a three-shot deficit when it counted during the middle of the round. He finished the tournament at 12-under for the weekend.

"It felt pretty similar to last (year's final round)," McIlroy told Jim Nantz of CBS Sports after the victory. "... after that, I played flawlessly."

As a result, McIlroy was presented with the green jacket by Augusta National chairman Fred Ridley during a ceremony that followed his postgame interview with Nantz.

The previous year’s winner usually presents the jacket, but because McIlroy won the event last year, it was the chairman.

Scheffler, who won the tournament in 2024, was among those who threatened the leader on Sunday evening, finishing the final round in second place, just one shot behind the winner.

McIlroy didn't want to overthink the process and any superstitions going into this year's event as the champion.

After the 18th hole, McIlroy was greeted by family and friends on his way toward the scoring area. Among that group were his mother and father.

"It was the second major win my mom was at," McIlroy said. "… there was a part of them that didn't want to come because they thought that was the reason last time, but I’m glad they were able to experience it this time.

The biggest stories, every morning. Stay up-to-date on all the key sports developments by subscribing to USA TODAY Sports' newsletter.

search_result.title: Nike posts Rory McIlroy ad after back-to-back Masters green jackets
search_result.text:
April 12, 2026, 7:48 p.m. ET

Rory McIlroy will need to come up with another Champions Dinner menu at Augusta National Golf Club.

The World No. 2 golfer defended his green jacket at Augusta National on Sunday by fighting through some adversity to win the 2026 Masters Tournament with a score of 12-under par. He finished a stroke ahead of Scottie Scheffler, who gave McIlroy a run for his money with a late surge on the back nine.

Nike released a sharp advertisement shortly after McIlroy's second consecutive Masters win on Sunday, one with plenty of references to the tournament at Augusta National.

"No alterations needed," Nike wrote on X (formerly Twitter). "@McIlroyRory proves he is tailored for the moment, securing back-to-back jackets at Augusta."

The win for McIlroy makes him just the fourth golfer to win back-to-back green jackets at Augusta National, and the first since 2002 when Tiger Woods won back-to-back Masters. It's the sixth career major championship for McIlroy, which is tied for the 12th most all-time.

He set the Masters record for the largest 36-hole lead at six strokes, and looked to be running away with the title. However, his third round on Saturday saw him lose that lead and head into Sunday's final round tied with Cameron Young for first place on the leaderboard. He'd face some adversity on the front nine — highlighted by a double bogey on the par-3 hole No. 6 — but overcame that with back-to-back birdies on holes No. 12 and No. 13 in the back nine.

Nike posts Rory McIlroy ad after back-to-back Masters win

search_result.title: Who missed cut at 2025 Masters? Brooks Koepka, Phil Mickelson ...
search_result.text:
Updated April 11, 2025, 10:40 p.m. ET

The sun set on Augusta National Golf Club on Friday evening, as did the championship hopes of 42 golfers.

The field at the 2025 Masters dwindled from 95 golfers to 53 after the cut line was set at 2-over on Friday, meaning dozens of big names and past champions will not make it to the weekend in Augusta, Georgia following lackluster first and second rounds.

Former Masters champions Dustin Johnson (2020), Sergio Garcia (2017) and Phil Mickelson (2004, 2006, 2010) are among the golfers that are done after 36 holes. Johnson missed the cut at the Masters for the second consecutive year, while Mickelson won't see the weekend in Augusta for the first time since 2016.

Garcia has now missed the cut at the Masters in three consecutive years. Meanwhile, Bernhard Langer missed the cut in his 40th and final Masters tournament of his career. The 67-year-old, two-time Masters champion landed on just the wrong side of the cut line after a bogey on hole No. 18.

Here's a look at the notable golfers that missed the cut:

OPINION: Dustin Johnson, once major lock, afterthought at Masters

Who missed the 2025 Masters cut?

The cut line fluctuated between 1-over to 2-over on Friday, but as the round ended, the cut line was set firmly at 2-over. Jordan Spieth (+2) and Jon Rahm (+2) live to fight another day despite flirting with the cut line. Everyone wasn't so lucky. Here are some notable golfers that missed the cut on Friday:

Dustin Johnson (+3)

Bernhard Langer (+3)

Tony Finau (+4)

Sergio Garcia (+4)

Fred Couples (+4)

Phil Mickelson (+5)

Brooks Koepka (+5)

Cameron Smith (+5)

Billy Horschel (+5)

Cameron Young (+7)

Will Zalatoris (+8)

Jose Luis Ballester (a) (+10)

Did Fred Couples miss cut at Masters 2025?

Yes.

Couples became the oldest player to make the cut at the Masters in 2023 at age 63, and he was on the verge of extending his record. After missing the cut in 2024, Couples returned strong to Augusta this year and opened the tournament by shooting 1-under 71 in his 40th start on Thursday, including an eagle on hole No. 14 from 191 yards out. His first-round score made him the second-oldest golfer to turn in a subpar round at the Masters.

Couples, 65, was on his way to playing the weekend, but a shaky performance on Friday landed him on the wrong side of the cut line. Couples hit six bogeys in the second round and finished 5-over on the day and 4-over on the tournament, two strokes over the cut line. Couples has previously made the cut 31 times at the Masters, second all-time to Jack Nicklaus (37). Couples won the Masters in 1992 and earned a lifetime exemption.

Couples’ record was challenged by 67-year-old Bernhard Langer, who could've become the oldest golfer to make the cut at the Masters if he made it into the weekend. But Langer finished 3-over for the tournament on Friday.

How to watch 2025 Masters: TV, streaming for weekend

Live coverage of the third and fourth rounds will broadcast on CBS, beginning at 2 p.m. ET, with early coverage on CBS Sports Network and Paramount+, starting at 10 a.m. ET.

The 2025 Masters can also be streamed via Masters.com or the Masters App, with particular feeds available for featured groups and featured holes at Augusta National Golf Club. search_result.title: Will Swalwell's name appear on June primary ballot in California?
search_result.text:
Updated April 14, 2026, 8:05 p.m. ET

This story has been updated with new information.

Rep. Eric Swalwell on Sunday announced plans to drop from California's governor’s race amid sexual assault allegations, bringing more disorder to an already chaotic election cycle.

Then, the following day, Swalwell announced that he would be resigning from Congress.

With Swalwell’s withdrawal, it brings into question whether or not his name will appear on the June primary ballot.

Here’s what to know about June's primary ballot after Swalwell's withdrawal, and when his seat for the 14th Congressional District of California will be replaced.

Will Swalwell’s name appear on June primary’s ballot?

The California Elections Codes does not provide candidates who have filed nominations documents for statewide offices, such as governor or lieutenant governor, with the option to withdraw those documents, a spokesperson for the California Secretary of State said.

As such, since Swalwell filed nomination documents, his name will appear on the June primary ballot, according to the spokesperson.

When will Swalwell's congressional vacancy be replaced?

On April 14, California Gov. Gavin Newsom announced that the state will hold a special election on August 18, 2026, to fill the vacancy in the office of the U.S. House of Representatives.

Swalwell faces accusations of sexual assault

On Friday afternoon, the San Francisco Chronicle published allegations of sexual assault from a former staffer against Swalwell, the leading Democratic candidate. CNN followed with sexual assault allegations from the same staffer and three other women who alleged other forms of sexual misconduct.

A wide array of pollical figures called for Swalwell to withdraw from the race.

Swalwell denied the allegations in a Friday video on social media, saying the claims were politically motivated. By Sunday evening, however, Swalwell announced plans to drop out of the race but continued to refute the allegations.

“To my family, staff, friends, and supporters, I am deeply sorry for mistakes in judgment I’ve made in my past,” Swalwell wrote in a post on X. “I will fight the serious, false allegations that have been made — but that’s my fight, not a campaign’s.”

The next day, Swalwell announced his plans to resign from Congress.

When is Eric Swalwell's last day as a congressman?

Though Swalwell announced his intent to resign from the U.S. House of Representatives, he did not provide a specific timeline for his final day.

“I will work with my staff in the coming days to ensure they are able, in my absence, to serve the needs of the good people of the 14th congressional district,” Swalwell wrote in a post on X.

What happens when a U.S. House of Representative member resigns?

Should a member of the U.S. House of Representatives resign, their seat becomes vacant, necessitating a special election to fill the vacancy, per the U.S. Constitution.

Unlike the Senate, the seat cannot be filled by a temporary appointment. The seat will remain open until a new member is elected in a special election called by the state’s governor. California Gov. Gavin Newsom has 14 days to proclaim a primary and then the general election date.

"Our office is reviewing the matter," Newsom’s office said in a statement provided to KTVU. "Once the seat is officially vacant, our office will make an official announcement."

Here’s what to expect after Newsom's call:

Primary Election: Typically held about 10 weeks after the governor’s call.

Typically held about 10 weeks after the governor’s call. Runoff: If no candidate wins outright, the top two advance to a general election roughly 10 weeks later.

If no candidate wins outright, the top two advance to a general election roughly 10 weeks later. Timeline: The process could take four to five months, leaving the Bay Area district without representation.

When is the governor's race?

Voters will choose the Golden State's next governor during California’s general election on Tuesday, Nov. 3.

Key dates for June primary
